title: Two-dimensional early exit optimisation of LLM inference
summary: We introduce a two-dimensional (2D) early exit strategy that coordinates layer-wise and sentence-wise exiting for classification tasks in large language models. By processing input incrementally sentence-by-sentence while progressively activating deeper layers, our method achieves multiplicative computational savings that exceed those from optimizing either dimension independently. Experimental evaluation across four state-of-the-art LLMs (Llama 3.1, Llama 3.2, Gemma, Qwen; 3B-8B parameters) on three sentiment classification datasets demonstrates additional speed-ups of 1.4--2.3$\times$ over optimal layer-wise early exit for simpler tasks with vanilla models, with graceful degradation on complex multi-class problems. Fine-tuning reduces but does not eliminate this advantage. The approach is model-agnostic, requires only lightweight classification adapters, and is orthogonal to complementary efficiency methods such as quantization and pruning. Our findings indicate that 2D early exit strategies excel when semantic information accumulates predictably across input structure, suggesting possible applicability to sequence-processing tasks beyond sentiment classification.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a 2D early exit strategy for LLM classification that jointly decides when to stop over layers and sentences, yielding multiplicative compute savings over standard layer-wise early exit.
AI.motivation: Running large language models for classification is expensive because all tokens typically traverse all layers. Existing early exit methods mostly operate along a single dimension, e.g., exiting at shallower layers, which leaves efficiency potential untapped. The authors aim to exploit both depth-wise and input-structure-wise redundancies, especially in tasks like sentiment analysis where useful semantics emerge early and in localized parts of the sequence.
AI.method: They design a two-dimensional early exit mechanism that (1) processes the input incrementally, sentence by sentence, and (2) progressively activates deeper transformer layers only as needed. At each step, the model can decide to stop processing either additional sentences or additional layers based on classification confidence, using lightweight classification adapters attached to intermediate representations. This strategy is model-agnostic and compatible with existing efficiency techniques such as quantization and pruning.
AI.result: On three sentiment classification datasets and four modern LLMs (Llama 3.1, Llama 3.2, Gemma, Qwen in the 3B–8B range), the method achieves 1.4–2.3× additional speed-ups compared to strong layer-wise early exit baselines on relatively simple tasks with standard (non-finetuned) models. On harder multi-class tasks, the computational advantage diminishes but the method still degrades gracefully in terms of both speed and accuracy. Fine-tuning narrows but does not erase the 2D early exit gains.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating exits across both model depth and input structure can yield multiplicative computational savings beyond what is possible by optimizing either axis alone. This 2D early exit paradigm is especially effective when semantic information accumulates in a predictable, structured way across the input, as in sentiment classification, and it is likely extendable to other sequence processing tasks. Because it is model-agnostic and uses only lightweight adapters, it can be combined with other efficiency methods to further reduce LLM inference cost.

title: Probing for Reading Times
summary: Probing has shown that language model representations encode rich linguistic information, but it remains unclear whether they also capture cognitive signals about human processing. In this work, we probe language model representations for human reading times. Using regularized linear regression on two eye-tracking corpora spanning five languages (English, Greek, Hebrew, Russian, and Turkish), we compare the representations from every model layer against scalar predictors -- surprisal, information value, and logit-lens surprisal. We find that the representations from early layers outperform surprisal in predicting early-pass measures such as first fixation and gaze duration. The concentration of predictive power in the early layers suggests that human-like processing signatures are captured by low-level structural or lexical representations, pointing to a functional alignment between model depth and the temporal stages of human reading. In contrast, for late-pass measures such as total reading time, scalar surprisal remains superior, despite its being a much more compressed representation. We also observe performance gains when using both surprisal and early-layer representations. Overall, we find that the best-performing predictor varies strongly depending on the language and eye-tracking measure.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether neural language model representations capture cognitive signals related to human reading times, beyond standard scalar surprisal measures.
AI.motivation: While probing has shown that language model layers encode rich linguistic information, it is still unclear if and how these representations align with human online processing, such as reading times measured via eye-tracking. The authors want to test whether internal model states can predict human reading behavior and how this compares to traditional information-theoretic predictors like surprisal.
AI.method: They use regularized linear regression to probe layer-wise representations from language models on two multi-lingual eye-tracking corpora (five languages: English, Greek, Hebrew, Russian, Turkish). For each layer, they evaluate how well its representations predict various eye-tracking measures (early-pass and late-pass) and compare this performance to scalar predictors such as surprisal, information value, and logit-lens surprisal. They also test combined models that use both surprisal and early-layer representations.
AI.result: Early-layer representations of language models outperform surprisal in predicting early-pass eye-tracking measures such as first fixation duration and gaze duration. Predictive power is concentrated in the early layers. For late-pass measures, such as total reading time, scalar surprisal remains a better predictor despite its compressed nature. Combining surprisal with early-layer representations further improves prediction, and the best predictor varies across languages and eye-tracking measures.
AI.conclusion: Language model representations, particularly from early layers, capture human-like processing signatures aligned with early stages of reading, suggesting a functional alignment between model depth and temporal stages of human reading. However, traditional scalar surprisal remains more effective for later, integrative reading measures. No single predictor is universally best; effectiveness depends on language and the specific eye-tracking metric.

title: Characterizing AlphaEarth Embedding Geometry for Agentic Environmental Reasoning
summary: Earth observation foundation models encode land surface information into dense embedding vectors, yet the geometric structure of these representations and its implications for downstream reasoning remain underexplored. We characterize the manifold geometry of Google AlphaEarth's 64-dimensional embeddings across 12.1 million Continental United States samples (2017--2023) and develop an agentic system that leverages this geometric understanding for environmental reasoning. The manifold is non-Euclidean: effective dimensionality is 13.3 (participation ratio) from 64 raw dimensions, with local intrinsic dimensionality of approximately 10. Tangent spaces rotate substantially, with 84\% of locations exceeding 60\textdegree{} and local-global alignment (mean$|\cosθ| = 0.17$) approaching the random baseline of 0.125. Supervised linear probes indicate that concept directions rotate across the manifold, and compositional vector arithmetic using both PCA-derived and probe-derived directions yields poor precision. Retrieval instead produces physically coherent results, with local geometry predicting retrieval coherence ($R^2 = 0.32$). Building on this characterization, we introduce an agentic system with nine specialized tools that decomposes environmental queries into reasoning chains over a FAISS-indexed embedding database. A five-condition ablation (120 queries, three complexity tiers) shows that embedding retrieval dominates response quality ($μ= 3.79 \pm 0.90$ vs.\ $3.03 \pm 0.77$ parametric-only; scale 1--5), with peak performance on multi-step comparisons ($μ= 4.28 \pm 0.43$). A cross-model benchmark show that geometric tools reduce Sonnet 4.5's score by 0.12 points but improve Opus 4.6's by 0.07, with Opus achieving higher geometric grounding (3.38 vs.\ 2.64), suggesting that the value of geometric characterization scales with the reasoning capability of the consuming model.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the geometry of AlphaEarth satellite-image embeddings and uses this understanding to build an agent system for environmental question answering that relies on retrieval over the embedding space.
AI.motivation: Foundation models for Earth observation produce powerful embeddings, but we do not yet understand their geometric structure or how that structure affects downstream reasoning and tool design. The authors want to characterize this geometry and test whether using it explicitly can improve environmental reasoning performance.
AI.method: They analyze 64D AlphaEarth embeddings for 12.1M US land samples (2017–2023) to estimate manifold dimensionality, local intrinsic dimensionality, and tangent-space alignment and rotation. They probe for how semantic directions behave, test compositional vector arithmetic, and relate local geometry to retrieval coherence. Then they build an agent system with nine specialized tools that answers environmental queries by decomposing them into reasoning chains grounded in a FAISS index over the embeddings. They run ablations across five conditions and three query-complexity tiers, and compare effects of geometric tools across two LLMs (Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.6).
AI.result: They find the manifold is strongly non-Euclidean: only ~13 effective dimensions out of 64, local intrinsic dimensionality ~10, and large rotations of tangent spaces with low local-global alignment. Semantic concept directions rotate across the manifold, and vector arithmetic (even using PCA/probe directions) performs poorly. Retrieval, however, yields physically coherent neighbors, and local geometry predicts retrieval coherence with R^2 = 0.32. In ablations on 120 queries, conditions with embedding retrieval substantially outperform parametric-only answering, especially for multi-step comparisons. Geometric tools slightly hurt Sonnet 4.5 but improve Opus 4.6 and increase its geometric grounding scores.
AI.conclusion: AlphaEarth embeddings live on a curved, low-effective-dimension manifold where global linear directions are unreliable and compositional vector arithmetic is weak, but local retrieval is strong and predictable from geometry. Explicitly leveraging this geometry in an agentic, retrieval-augmented system significantly improves environmental reasoning, particularly for stronger base models, indicating that geometric characterization is most beneficial when paired with capable LLM reasoners.

title: Scripts Through Time: A Survey of the Evolving Role of Transliteration in NLP
summary: Cross-lingual transfer in NLP is often hindered by the ``script barrier'' where differences in writing systems inhibit transfer learning between languages. Transliteration, the process of converting the script, has emerged as a powerful technique to bridge this gap by increasing lexical overlap. This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the application of transliteration in cross-lingual NLP. We present a taxonomy of key motivations to utilize transliterations in language models, and provide an overview of different approaches of incorporating transliterations as input. We analyze the evolution and effectiveness of these methods, discussing the critical trade-offs involved, and contextualize their need in modern LLMs. The review explores various settings that show how transliteration is beneficial, including handling code-mixed text, leveraging language family relatedness, and pragmatic gains in inference efficiency. Based on this analysis, we provide concrete recommendations for researchers on selecting and implementing the most appropriate transliteration strategy based on their specific language, task, and resource constraints.

AI.tldr: Survey of how transliteration helps cross-lingual NLP by overcoming script barriers, categorizing motivations and methods, analyzing trade-offs, and giving practical recommendations.
AI.motivation: Cross-lingual transfer in NLP is limited when languages use different writing systems, reducing lexical overlap and transfer effectiveness. Transliteration can bridge this gap, but practices are scattered and not systematically understood, creating a need for a structured overview and guidance.
AI.method: Conduct a comprehensive survey of prior work that incorporates transliteration in cross-lingual NLP, propose a taxonomy of motivations, categorize methods of incorporating transliterations into models, and analyze these methods in terms of evolution, effectiveness, and trade-offs, especially in the context of modern LLMs.
AI.result: The survey identifies major motivations for using transliteration (e.g., overcoming script barriers, handling code-mixing, exploiting language relatedness, and improving inference efficiency), categorizes input and modeling strategies for transliteration, and evaluates their relative strengths and weaknesses across various scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Transliteration is a generally effective tool for improving cross-lingual NLP in many settings, particularly where script differences hinder transfer. The paper offers concrete, scenario-based recommendations to help researchers choose and implement appropriate transliteration strategies based on language pairs, tasks, and resource constraints, and clarifies when transliteration is most and least beneficial in modern LLM pipelines.

title: On Solving the Multiple Variable Gapped Longest Common Subsequence Problem
summary: This paper addresses the Variable Gapped Longest Common Subsequence (VGLCS) problem, a generalization of the classical LCS problem involving flexible gap constraints between consecutive solutions' characters. The problem arises in molecular sequence comparison, where structural distance constraints between residues must be respected, and in time-series analysis where events are required to occur within specified temporal delays. We propose a search framework based on the root-based state graph representation, in which the state space comprises a generally large number of rooted state subgraphs. To cope with the resulting combinatorial explosion, an iterative beam search strategy is employed, dynamically maintaining a global pool of promising candidate root nodes, enabling effective control of diversification across iterations. To exploit the search for high-quality solutions, several known heuristics from the LCS literature are utilized into the standalone beam search procedure. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive computational study on the VGLCS problem comprising 320 synthetic instances with up to 10 input sequences and up to 500 characters. Experimental results show robustness of the designed approach over the baseline beam search in comparable runtimes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an iterative beam search framework using a root-based state graph to effectively solve the Variable Gapped Longest Common Subsequence (VGLCS) problem, demonstrating robust performance on synthetic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The VGLCS problem extends the classical Longest Common Subsequence problem by allowing flexible, variable gaps between matched characters, which is important in applications like molecular sequence comparison (where structural distance constraints between residues must be met) and time-series analysis (where events must occur within specific time delays). Existing methods and studies for this generalized problem are limited, motivating a more systematic and computationally effective approach.
AI.method: The authors design a search framework grounded in a root-based state graph representation, where each state corresponds to a rooted state subgraph. Because this representation creates a very large search space, they apply an iterative beam search strategy that keeps a global pool of promising root nodes to balance intensification and diversification. Additionally, they integrate several established heuristics from the LCS literature into the beam search to better guide the search toward high-quality VGLCS solutions.
AI.result: On a benchmark consisting of 320 synthetic VGLCS instances with up to 10 input sequences and 500 characters each, the proposed method performs robustly and outperforms a baseline beam search in terms of solution quality while maintaining comparable runtimes.
AI.conclusion: The study provides what appears to be the first comprehensive computational investigation of the VGLCS problem and shows that a root-based state graph combined with iterative beam search and adapted LCS heuristics is an effective and robust approach for solving VGLCS instances of practical size.

title: Investigating Counterfactual Unfairness in LLMs towards Identities through Humor
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference. The first two authors contributed equally. The last three authors are co-corresponding authors
summary: Humor holds up a mirror to social perception: what we find funny often reflects who we are and how we judge others. When language models engage with humor, their reactions expose the social assumptions they have internalized from training data. In this paper, we investigate counterfactual unfairness through humor by observing how the model's responses change when we swap who speaks and who is addressed while holding other factors constant. Our framework spans three tasks: humor generation refusal, speaker intention inference, and relational/societal impact prediction, covering both identity-agnostic humor and identity-specific disparagement humor. We introduce interpretable bias metrics that capture asymmetric patterns under identity swaps. Experiments across state-of-the-art models reveal consistent relational disparities: jokes told by privileged speakers are refused up to 67.5% more often, judged as malicious 64.7% more frequently, and rated up to 1.5 points higher in social harm on a 5-point scale. These patterns highlight how sensitivity and stereotyping coexist in generative models, complicating efforts toward fairness and cultural alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how large language models respond to humor to reveal embedded social biases, using identity swaps to detect counterfactual unfairness.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how social biases and assumptions learned from training data manifest in language models’ handling of humor, particularly when different social identities are involved.
AI.method: Define a counterfactual framework that swaps speaker and target identities while holding other factors constant, and evaluate models on three tasks: humor-generation refusal, inferred speaker intention, and predicted relational/societal impact. Develop interpretable bias metrics that measure asymmetries in model behavior under identity swaps, and run experiments on state-of-the-art language models.
AI.result: Across multiple leading models, the authors find systematic relational disparities: jokes from privileged speakers are more frequently refused, more often judged as malicious, and assessed as causing greater social harm, with quantitative differences up to 67.5% in refusal rates, 64.7% in maliciousness judgments, and 1.5 points on a 5-point harm scale.
AI.conclusion: Language models encode complex, asymmetric sensitivities around humor that depend on speaker and target identities, indicating counterfactual unfairness. These results show that heightened sensitivity and stereotyping can coexist, complicating attempts to make generative models both fair and culturally well-aligned.

title: Beyond One Output: Visualizing and Comparing Distributions of Language Model Generations
summary: Users typically interact with and evaluate language models via single outputs, but each output is just one sample from a broad distribution of possible completions. This interaction hides distributional structure such as modes, uncommon edge cases, and sensitivity to small prompt changes, leading users to over-generalize from anecdotes when iterating on prompts for open-ended tasks. Informed by a formative study with researchers who use LMs (n=13) examining when stochasticity matters in practice, how they reason about distributions over language, and where current workflows break down, we introduce GROVE. GROVE is an interactive visualization that represents multiple LM generations as overlapping paths through a text graph, revealing shared structure, branching points, and clusters while preserving access to raw outputs. We evaluate across three crowdsourced user studies (N=47, 44, and 40 participants) targeting complementary distributional tasks. Our results support a hybrid workflow: graph summaries improve structural judgments such as assessing diversity, while direct output inspection remains stronger for detail-oriented questions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents GROVE, an interactive visualization tool that shows the distribution of language model (LM) outputs as overlapping paths in a text graph, helping users understand structural patterns and variability beyond single-output inspection.
AI.motivation: Users typically see only one or a few samples from a language model, which hides the broader distribution of possible outputs, including modes, rare behaviors, and sensitivity to prompt variations. This can cause over-generalization from anecdotal examples and inefficiencies when iterating prompts for open-ended tasks. The authors want to make the distributional structure of LM outputs visible and usable in practice.
AI.method: The authors first conduct a formative study with 13 LM-using researchers to understand when stochasticity matters, how they conceptualize distributions over language, and where current workflows fail. Based on these insights, they design GROVE, an interactive visualization that encodes multiple LM generations as overlapping paths in a text graph. The graph highlights shared prefixes, branching points, and clusters of similar continuations while allowing inspection of individual raw outputs. They then run three crowdsourced user studies (with 47, 44, and 40 participants) on complementary distributional reasoning tasks to evaluate how graph-based summaries compare to traditional list-based output inspection.
AI.result: Across the three user studies, participants using GROVE’s graph visualizations show improved performance on structural distributional tasks, such as judging output diversity or identifying major modes and branches, compared to those relying on raw-output lists alone. However, for detail-oriented questions that require close reading of specific completions, traditional direct inspection of outputs still performs better or remains necessary. These findings indicate that each representation has strengths depending on the task.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LM interaction benefits from a hybrid workflow: visualization-based graph summaries like GROVE are effective for understanding and reasoning about the global structure of output distributions, while direct inspection of individual outputs remains superior for fine-grained, content-specific questions. Making distributions visible helps users avoid over-generalizing from anecdotes and supports more informed prompt design and analysis, but should complement rather than replace existing practices.

title: Remask, Don't Replace: Token-to-Mask Refinement in Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models such as LLaDA2.1 rely on Token-to-Token (T2T) editing to correct their own generation errors: whenever a different token crosses a confidence threshold, the committed token is overwritten. We identify three structural failure modes of this rule. The trigger cannot fire when no single alternative is confident enough; the replacement is computed under a context that may itself contain errors; and the uniform perturbations used to train the T2T stream do not resemble the coherent, semantically plausible mistakes that the model actually makes at inference. As an alternative, we propose Token-to-Mask (T2M) remasking. Rather than overwriting a suspect token with a new guess, T2M resets the position to the mask state, so that the next denoising step re-predicts it from an in-distribution context. The method is training-free, modifies only the editing rule, and introduces no new parameters. We pair it with three detection heuristics and give a short theoretical account of why a mask is a better conditioning signal than an erroneous token. Across 8 benchmarks, T2M improves accuracy on tasks that require exact token-level output. Its largest gain is +5.92 points on CMATH, where we attribute 79.9% of baseline errors to last-mile corruption (correct reasoning followed by a garbled final answer); T2M repairs 41.3% of these cases.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies key failure modes in existing Token-to-Token (T2T) editing for masked diffusion language models and proposes a simple, training-free Token-to-Mask (T2M) remasking rule that treats low-confidence tokens as masks rather than overwriting them with new guesses, yielding notable accuracy gains on exact token-level tasks.
AI.motivation: Masked diffusion language models depend on Token-to-Token editing to fix their own generation errors, but this mechanism can fail when no alternative token is sufficiently confident, when replacement is computed on an already corrupted context, and when training perturbations do not match realistic inference-time mistakes. These structural issues especially hurt tasks where exact token accuracy matters, such as math problems whose reasoning is correct but final answer tokens are corrupted.
AI.method: The authors replace the standard Token-to-Token overwriting rule with Token-to-Mask remasking: instead of substituting a low-confidence token with another candidate token, they reset that position to a mask so the next denoising step predicts it from an in-distribution masked context. This approach is training-free, requires no new parameters, and only changes the inference-time editing rule. It is combined with three heuristics for detecting suspect tokens, and supported by a theoretical argument that conditioning on a mask is more reliable than conditioning on an erroneous token.
AI.result: Across eight benchmarks, the T2M remasking rule improves accuracy on tasks requiring exact token-level correctness, with the largest reported gain of +5.92 points on the CMATH benchmark. On CMATH, they find that 79.9% of baseline errors are due to last-mile corruption, where reasoning is correct but the final answer tokens are wrong; T2M fixes 41.3% of these last-mile errors.
AI.conclusion: Token-to-Mask remasking is a simple, training-free modification to the editing rule in masked diffusion language models that mitigates structural weaknesses of Token-to-Token editing. By remasking low-confidence tokens and re-predicting them in a clean masked context, T2M yields substantial improvements on exact token-level benchmarks, particularly by repairing last-mile answer corruptions in math tasks.

title: ARES: Adaptive Red-Teaming and End-to-End Repair of Policy-Reward System
summary:
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is central to aligning Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it introduces a critical vulnerability: an imperfect Reward Model (RM) can become a single point of failure when it fails to penalize unsafe behaviors. While existing red-teaming approaches primarily target policy-level weaknesses, they overlook what we term systemic weaknesses cases where both the core LLM and the RM fail in tandem.
  We present ARES, a framework that systematically discovers and mitigates such dual vulnerabilities. ARES employs a ``Safety Mentor'' that dynamically composes semantically coherent adversarial prompts by combining structured component types (topics, personas, tactics, goals) and generates corresponding malicious and safe responses. This dual-targeting approach exposes weaknesses in both the core LLM and the RM simultaneously. Using the vulnerabilities gained, ARES implements a two-stage repair process: first fine-tuning the RM to better detect harmful content, then leveraging the improved RM to optimize the core model. Experiments across multiple adversarial safety benchmarks demonstrate that ARES substantially enhances safety robustness while preserving model capabilities, establishing a new paradigm for comprehensive RLHF safety alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ARES, a framework that finds and fixes cases where both a language model and its reward model simultaneously fail to detect unsafe content in RLHF, significantly improving safety robustness without hurting capabilities.
AI.motivation: In RLHF, the reward model becomes a single point of failure: if it mis-scores harmful outputs, the final aligned model can systematically produce unsafe responses. Existing red-teaming focuses mostly on the final policy (the LLM) and misses systemic failures where both the LLM and the reward model fail together. The authors are motivated to identify and repair these joint vulnerabilities in a structured, scalable way.
AI.method: The authors propose ARES, which uses a "Safety Mentor" to automatically construct adversarial prompts by composing structured components (topics, personas, tactics, goals) into semantically coherent attacks. For each prompt, it generates paired malicious and safe responses, then tests both the core LLM and the reward model to uncover cases where they jointly mis-handle safety. ARES then performs a two-stage repair: (1) fine-tune the reward model on discovered failures so it better detects harmful content; (2) use the improved reward model to re-optimize the core LLM using RLHF or related optimization, thereby propagating the safety improvements to the policy.
AI.result: Across several adversarial safety benchmarks, ARES discovers dual vulnerabilities that prior approaches miss and, after the two-stage repair, significantly improves robustness to unsafe prompts. The improved models show higher resistance to red-teaming while maintaining overall task performance, outperforming baseline safety alignment and red-teaming methods.
AI.conclusion: ARES demonstrates that targeting both the core model and the reward model simultaneously exposes systemic safety weaknesses that conventional policy-only red-teaming overlooks. By repairing the reward model first and then re-optimizing the policy, the framework achieves stronger, more comprehensive RLHF safety alignment without sacrificing capabilities, suggesting a new direction for building safer large language models.

title: Syntax as a Rosetta Stone: Universal Dependencies for In-Context Coptic Translation
summary: Low-resource machine translation requires methods that differ from those used for high-resource languages. This paper proposes a novel in-context learning approach to support low-resource machine translation of the Coptic language to English, with syntactic augmentation from Universal Dependencies parses of input sentences. Building on existing work using bilingual dictionaries to support inference for vocabulary items, we add several representations of syntactic analyses to our inputs , specifically exploring the inclusion of raw parser outputs, verbalizations of parses in plain English, and targeted instructions of difficult constructions identified in sub-trees and how they can be translated. Our results show that while syntactic information alone is not as useful as dictionary-based glosses, combining retrieved dictionary items with syntactic information achieves significant gains across model sizes, achieving new state-of-the-art translation results for Coptic.

AI.tldr: They enhance low-resource Coptic→English translation by adding syntactic information from dependency parses into in-context learning prompts, combined with dictionary glosses, achieving new state of the art.
AI.motivation: Low-resource languages like Coptic lack large parallel corpora, so standard high-resource MT methods perform poorly. Prior in-context MT for Coptic relies mainly on bilingual dictionaries for unknown words, but ignores syntax, even though syntactic clues could help models interpret complex constructions and word order variations when data is scarce. The paper aims to see whether explicit syntactic augmentation can close this gap and improve translation quality.
AI.method: They use in-context learning with large language models for Coptic→English translation and augment the Coptic input with syntactic information derived from Universal Dependencies parses. Several prompt formats are explored: (1) raw UD parser outputs attached to the source sentence; (2) verbalizations of the dependency trees as human-readable English descriptions; and (3) focused instructional snippets that highlight difficult syntactic subtrees and explain how such constructions should be translated. They integrate these syntactic augmentations with existing dictionary-based gloss retrieval for vocabulary support and compare different model sizes and combinations of signals.
AI.result: Syntactic information by itself improves translation only modestly and remains less beneficial than dictionary-based glosses. However, when syntactic augmentations are combined with retrieved dictionary items, translation quality improves substantially across multiple model sizes, yielding significant gains over baselines and establishing new state-of-the-art performance for Coptic→English MT on the evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: For very low-resource MT, syntax-aware prompting is most effective when used in conjunction with lexical support rather than as a standalone signal. Explicit dependency-based syntactic cues can help large language models better exploit dictionary glosses and handle complex constructions, leading to state-of-the-art Coptic→English translation. The work suggests that integrating structured linguistic analyses into in-context learning is a promising direction for other low-resource languages as well.

title: AI scientists produce results without reasoning scientifically
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based systems are increasingly deployed to conduct scientific research autonomously, yet whether their reasoning adheres to the epistemic norms that make scientific inquiry self-correcting is poorly understood. Here, we evaluate LLM-based scientific agents across eight domains, spanning workflow execution to hypothesis-driven inquiry, through more than 25,000 agent runs and two complementary lenses: (i) a systematic performance analysis that decomposes the contributions of the base model and the agent scaffold, and (ii) a behavioral analysis of the epistemological structure of agent reasoning. We observe that the base model is the primary determinant of both performance and behavior, accounting for 41.4% of explained variance versus 1.5% for the scaffold. Across all configurations, evidence is ignored in 68% of traces, refutation-driven belief revision occurs in 26%, and convergent multi-test evidence is rare. The same reasoning pattern appears whether the agent executes a computational workflow or conducts hypothesis-driven inquiry. They persist even when agents receive near-complete successful reasoning trajectories as context, and the resulting unreliability compounds across repeated trials in epistemically demanding domains. Thus, current LLM-based agents execute scientific workflows but do not exhibit the epistemic patterns that characterize scientific reasoning. Outcome-based evaluation cannot detect these failures, and scaffold engineering alone cannot repair them. Until reasoning itself becomes a training target, the scientific knowledge produced by such agents cannot be justified by the process that generated it.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically tests how well LLM-based scientific agents follow core norms of scientific reasoning and finds that they systematically fail to do so, even when they can execute workflows and achieve correct outcomes.
AI.motivation: LLM agents are starting to perform end-to-end scientific tasks (e.g., running analyses, designing experiments), but it is unclear whether their internal reasoning resembles the self-correcting, evidence-based processes that underpin trustworthy science. Existing evaluations mostly look at final accuracy or task completion, not at whether agents revise beliefs when refuted, integrate multiple lines of evidence, or avoid ignoring disconfirming data. The paper aims to fill this gap to assess whether knowledge produced by such agents is epistemically trustworthy, not just instrumentally useful.
AI.method: The authors construct LLM-based scientific agents that operate across eight scientific domains, from executing computational workflows to performing hypothesis-driven inquiry. They run over 25,000 agent executions with different base LLMs and different agent scaffolds. They then analyze: (1) performance, by decomposing how much variance in success is explained by the underlying base model versus the agent scaffold; and (2) behavior, by coding and quantifying the epistemic structure of reasoning traces, focusing on how agents handle evidence (whether they ignore it, revise beliefs upon refutation, or integrate convergent evidence). They also test robustness by providing near-complete successful reasoning trajectories as context and examining repeated trials in more demanding domains.
AI.result: The base LLM largely determines both performance and reasoning behavior, explaining 41.4% of the variance in outcomes, compared to only 1.5% attributable to the agent scaffold. Across all tested configurations, agents ignore available evidence in 68% of reasoning traces. Explicit, refutation-driven belief revision happens in only 26% of cases, and instances where agents integrate convergent evidence from multiple tests are rare. These problematic reasoning patterns are consistent whether agents are running computational workflows or engaging in hypothesis-driven inquiry. The failures persist even when agents are given near-complete, successful reasoning trajectories as context, and the unreliability increases when tasks require sustained, epistemically demanding reasoning over repeated trials.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM-based scientific agents can carry out scientific workflows and sometimes obtain correct answers, but they do not reliably follow the epistemic norms that make scientific reasoning self-correcting—such as appropriately using evidence, revising beliefs after refutation, and integrating multiple lines of support. Because these deficits are rooted in the base model rather than the scaffolding, better agent design alone cannot fix them, and purely outcome-based evaluations will miss the underlying epistemic failures. The authors argue that for the scientific knowledge generated by such systems to be epistemically justified, reasoning itself must become an explicit target in model training, not just task performance or output quality.

title: Model-Agnostic Meta Learning for Class Imbalance Adaptation
summary: Class imbalance is a widespread challenge in NLP tasks, significantly hindering robust performance across diverse domains and applications. We introduce Hardness-Aware Meta-Resample (HAMR), a unified framework that adaptively addresses both class imbalance and data difficulty. HAMR employs bi-level optimizations to dynamically estimate instance-level weights that prioritize genuinely challenging samples and minority classes, while a neighborhood-aware resampling mechanism amplifies training focus on hard examples and their semantically similar neighbors. We validate HAMR on six imbalanced datasets covering multiple tasks and spanning biomedical, disaster response, and sentiment domains. Experimental results show that HAMR achieves substantial improvements for minority classes and consistently outperforms strong baselines. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate that our proposed modules synergistically contribute to performance gains and highlight HAMR as a flexible and generalizable approach for class imbalance adaptation. Code is available at https://github.com/trust-nlp/ImbalanceLearning.

AI.tldr: Proposes HAMR, a hardness-aware meta-resampling framework to address class imbalance and data difficulty in NLP.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance degrades NLP performance, especially for minority classes and hard samples, and existing methods do not adequately or adaptively handle both class imbalance and instance difficulty across domains.
AI.method: Introduces HAMR, which uses bi-level optimization to learn instance-level weights that emphasize genuinely hard and minority-class examples, and a neighborhood-aware resampling mechanism that further focuses learning on hard examples and their semantically similar neighbors, forming a unified hardness- and imbalance-aware training framework.
AI.result: Across six imbalanced datasets in biomedical, disaster response, and sentiment analysis tasks, HAMR yields significant improvements on minority-class performance and consistently outperforms strong baseline methods; ablation studies show that each proposed module contributes to the overall gains.
AI.conclusion: HAMR is an effective, flexible, and generalizable framework for adapting to class imbalance in NLP by jointly modeling class frequency and example hardness via meta-learned weights and neighborhood-aware resampling.

title: An Empirical Study of Multi-Generation Sampling for Jailbreak Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Detecting jailbreak behaviour in large language models remains challenging, particularly when strongly aligned models produce harmful outputs only rarely. In this work, we present an empirical study of output based jailbreak detection under realistic conditions using the JailbreakBench Behaviors dataset and multiple generator models with varying alignment strengths. We evaluate both a lexical TF-IDF detector and a generation inconsistency based detector across different sampling budgets. Our results show that single output evaluation systematically underestimates jailbreak vulnerability, as increasing the number of sampled generations reveals additional harmful behaviour. The most significant improvements occur when moving from a single generation to moderate sampling, while larger sampling budgets yield diminishing returns. Cross generator experiments demonstrate that detection signals partially generalise across models, with stronger transfer observed within related model families. A category level analysis further reveals that lexical detectors capture a mixture of behavioural signals and topic specific cues, rather than purely harmful behaviour. Overall, our findings suggest that moderate multi sample auditing provides a more reliable and practical approach for estimating model vulnerability and improving jailbreak detection in large language models. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies how to better detect jailbreak behavior in large language models using multiple samples per prompt instead of a single output.
AI.motivation: Detecting jailbreaks is hard when well-aligned models rarely produce harmful outputs, so single-response checks underestimate true vulnerability. The authors want a realistic, quantitative understanding of how output-based detectors perform under different sampling regimes and across models, to guide practical auditing strategies.
AI.method: They use the JailbreakBench Behaviors dataset and several LLMs with different alignment levels. They test two output-based detectors: (1) a lexical TF-IDF classifier and (2) a detector based on generation inconsistency. They vary the number of sampled generations (sampling budget) per prompt and run both within-model and cross-model (transfer) experiments. They also perform category-level analyses of what signals lexical detectors actually capture.
AI.result: They find that evaluating only a single generation substantially underestimates jailbreak risk. As the number of sampled outputs increases, more harmful behavior is revealed, with the biggest gains from going from 1 sample to a moderate number; further increases show diminishing returns. Detection signals transfer partially across models, especially within related model families. The lexical detector is shown to respond to both genuine behavioral cues and topic-specific lexical patterns, not purely to harmfulness itself.
AI.conclusion: Moderate multi-sample auditing—sampling several outputs per prompt rather than just one—gives a more accurate and practical estimate of jailbreak vulnerability. Output-based detectors can generalize somewhat across models but are confounded by topic cues, implying that auditors should use multiple samples and be cautious about interpreting lexical detector scores as pure measures of harmful behavior.

title: Human-Guided Harm Recovery for Computer Use Agents
summary: As LM agents gain the ability to execute actions on real computer systems, we need ways to not only prevent harmful actions at scale but also effectively remediate harm when prevention fails. We formalize a solution to this neglected challenge in post-execution safeguards as harm recovery: the problem of optimally steering an agent from a harmful state back to a safe one in alignment with human preferences. We ground preference-aligned recovery through a formative user study that identifies valued recovery dimensions and produces a natural language rubric. Our dataset of 1,150 pairwise judgments reveals context-dependent shifts in attribute importance, such as preferences for pragmatic, targeted strategies over comprehensive long-term approaches. We operationalize these learned insights in a reward model, re-ranking multiple candidate recovery plans generated by an agent scaffold at test time. To evaluate recovery capabilities systematically, we introduce BackBench, a benchmark of 50 computer-use tasks that test an agent's ability to recover from harmful states. Human evaluation shows our reward model scaffold yields higher-quality recovery trajectories than base agents and rubric-based scaffolds. Together, these contributions lay the foundation for a new class of agent safety methods -- ones that confront harm not only by preventing it, but by navigating its aftermath with alignment and intent.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and studies "harm recovery" for language-model-based computer agents: steering an agent from a harmful system state back to a safe one, aligned with human preferences.
AI.motivation: As language model agents gain the ability to act on real computer systems, existing safety work focuses heavily on preventing harmful actions but pays little attention to what to do once harm has occurred. There is a need for systematic, preference-aligned methods to remediate or recover from harmful states, because prevention will sometimes fail and agents must then handle the aftermath safely and effectively.
AI.method: 1) Conceptually define harm recovery as moving from harmful to safe states according to human preferences. 2) Conduct a formative user study to elicit what people value in recovery behaviors, resulting in a natural-language rubric describing recovery dimensions. 3) Collect a dataset of 1,150 pairwise human judgments over recovery plans, analyzing how preferences shift with context (e.g., preferring pragmatic, targeted fixes versus broader long-term strategies). 4) Train a reward model on these pairwise judgments to capture preference patterns. 5) Use this reward model at test time to re-rank multiple candidate recovery plans produced by an LM agent scaffold. 6) Build BackBench, a benchmark of 50 realistic computer-use tasks specifically designed to evaluate an agent’s ability to recover from harmful states. 7) Compare the reward-model-based scaffold to baseline agents and rubric-based scaffolds via human evaluation.
AI.result: The user study surfaces key recovery dimensions and demonstrates that people’s preferences over recovery strategies depend on context (e.g., prioritizing direct, efficient fixes in some scenarios). The trained reward model successfully encodes these nuanced preferences and, when used to re-rank candidate recovery trajectories, leads to empirically better recovery behavior. On the BackBench benchmark, human evaluation shows that the reward-model-guided scaffold generates higher-quality recovery trajectories than both a base agent without such guidance and a heuristic rubric-based scaffold.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes harm recovery as a distinct, important problem for LM agent safety and provides initial tools to study and improve it. By combining human preference data, a learned reward model, and a dedicated benchmark (BackBench), the paper demonstrates that post-execution safeguards can be made more aligned and effective. This lays groundwork for a new class of safety methods that address not just preventing harm, but also guiding agents through the process of repairing and mitigating harm after it occurs.

title: Mango: Multi-Agent Web Navigation via Global-View Optimization
summary: Existing web agents typically initiate exploration from the root URL, which is inefficient for complex websites with deep hierarchical structures. Without a global view of the website's structure, agents frequently fall into navigation traps, explore irrelevant branches, or fail to reach target information within a limited budget. We propose Mango, a multi-agent web navigation method that leverages the website structure to dynamically determine optimal starting points. We formulate URL selection as a multi-armed bandit problem and employ Thompson Sampling to adaptively allocate the navigation budget across candidate URLs. Furthermore, we introduce an episodic memory component to store navigation history, enabling the agent to learn from previous attempts. Experiments on WebVoyager demonstrate that Mango achieves a success rate of 63.6% when using GPT-5-mini, outperforming the best baseline by 7.3%. Furthermore, on WebWalkerQA, Mango attains a 52.5% success rate, surpassing the best baseline by 26.8%. We also demonstrate the generalizability of Mango using both open-source and closed-source models as backbones. Our data and code are open-source and available at https://github.com/VichyTong/Mango.

AI.tldr: Mango is a multi-agent web navigation framework that treats URL selection as a multi-armed bandit problem and uses Thompson Sampling plus episodic memory to choose good starting URLs and improve success on complex websites.
AI.motivation: Web agents that always start from the homepage waste budget on deep or complex sites, often get stuck, and lack a global view of the website structure, leading to low task success rates on realistic web navigation benchmarks.
AI.method: Mango decomposes web navigation into multiple agents starting from different URLs. It models URL choice as a multi-armed bandit, using Thompson Sampling to allocate limited navigation budget across candidate starting URLs based on their estimated usefulness. An episodic memory module records past navigation trajectories and outcomes, so the system can learn which areas of the site are promising or unproductive, influencing future URL selection and exploration.
AI.result: On the WebVoyager benchmark, Mango with GPT-5-mini achieves a 63.6% success rate, 7.3% higher than the strongest baseline. On WebWalkerQA, it reaches 52.5% success, outperforming the best baseline by 26.8%. It shows consistent gains when instantiated with both open-source and closed-source LLM backbones, demonstrating robustness and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging website structure to choose better starting points, formulating URL selection as a bandit problem with Thompson Sampling, and incorporating episodic memory significantly improve web agent performance on complex navigation tasks. Mango generalizes across models and benchmarks and is released with open-source data and code.

title: From Natural Language to Executable Narsese: A Neuro-Symbolic Benchmark and Pipeline for Reasoning with NARS
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are highly capable at language generation, but they remain unreliable when reasoning requires explicit symbolic structure, multi-step inference, and interpretable uncertainty. This paper presents a neuro-symbolic framework for translating natural-language reasoning problems into executable formal representations using first-order logic (FOL) and Narsese, the language of the Non-Axiomatic Reasoning System (NARS). To support this direction, we introduce NARS-Reasoning-v0.1, a benchmark of natural-language reasoning problems paired with FOL forms, executable Narsese programs, and three gold labels: True, False, and Uncertain. We develop a deterministic compilation pipeline from FOL to executable Narsese and validate retained examples through runtime execution in OpenNARS for Applications (ONA), ensuring that the symbolic targets are not only syntactically well formed but also behaviorally aligned with the intended answer. We further present Language-Structured Perception (LSP), a formulation in which an LLM is trained to produce reasoning-relevant symbolic structure rather than only a final verbal response. As an initial proof of concept, we also train and release a Phi-2 LoRA adapter on NARS-Reasoning-v0.1 for three-label reasoning classification, showing that the benchmark can support supervised adaptation in addition to executable evaluation. Overall, the paper positions executable symbolic generation and execution-based validation as a practical path toward more reliable neuro-symbolic reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neuro-symbolic framework that converts natural-language reasoning problems into executable logical forms (FOL and Narsese), introduces a benchmark (NARS-Reasoning-v0.1), and shows that generating and executing symbolic structures can improve reliability in LLM-based reasoning.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong at fluent language generation but unreliable when tasks require explicit symbolic structure, multi-step reasoning, and calibrated uncertainty. There is a need for systems that can handle formal reasoning with interpretable internal structure and support verifiable, executable validation of their reasoning steps, instead of only producing final verbal answers.
AI.method: 1) Construct NARS-Reasoning-v0.1, a dataset of natural-language reasoning problems paired with first-order logic (FOL) representations, executable Narsese programs, and three-label ground truth (True/False/Uncertain). 2) Design a deterministic compilation pipeline that translates FOL into executable Narsese and validate the resulting programs by running them in OpenNARS for Applications (ONA) to ensure behavioral correctness. 3) Propose Language-Structured Perception (LSP), where an LLM is trained to output reasoning-relevant symbolic structures rather than only textual answers. 4) Train and release a Phi-2 LoRA adapter on the benchmark for three-way reasoning classification to demonstrate supervised adaptation using the dataset.
AI.result: The authors successfully build the NARS-Reasoning-v0.1 benchmark and show that FOL forms can be reliably compiled into Narsese programs whose execution in ONA matches intended labels. They demonstrate that LLMs can be trained to produce useful symbolic structures for reasoning tasks and that a Phi-2 LoRA adapter trained on the benchmark achieves effective three-label reasoning classification performance, indicating that the benchmark is suitable for both executable and supervised evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Executable symbolic generation combined with execution-based validation offers a promising and practical route toward more reliable neuro-symbolic reasoning systems. By providing a benchmark, compilation pipeline, and proof-of-concept LLM adaptation, the paper argues that training models to output interpretable, executable reasoning structures—rather than only final answers—can improve robustness and trustworthiness in complex reasoning tasks.

title: Experiments or Outcomes? Probing Scientific Feasibility in Large Language Models
summary: Scientific feasibility assessment asks whether a claim is consistent with established knowledge and whether experimental evidence could support or refute it. We frame feasibility assessment as a diagnostic reasoning task in which, given a hypothesis, a model predicts feasible or infeasible and justifies its decision. We evaluate large language models (LLMs) under controlled knowledge conditions (hypothesis-only, with experiments, with outcomes, or both) and probe robustness by progressively removing portions of the experimental and/or outcome context. Across multiple LLMs and two datasets, providing outcome evidence is generally more reliable than providing experiment descriptions. Outcomes tend to improve accuracy beyond what internal knowledge alone provides, whereas experimental text can be brittle and may degrade performance when the context is incomplete. These findings clarify when experimental evidence benefits LLM-based feasibility assessment and when it introduces fragility.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models judge whether scientific hypotheses are feasible, especially when given experimental descriptions and outcomes.
AI.motivation: Researchers and reviewers need to quickly assess whether scientific claims are plausible and testable, but this reasoning is complex and time‑consuming. As LLMs are increasingly used in scientific workflows, it is important to understand whether and how they can reliably evaluate the feasibility of scientific hypotheses, and what kinds of evidence (prior knowledge, experiment descriptions, outcomes) help or harm their performance.
AI.method: The authors formalize feasibility assessment as a binary diagnostic reasoning task: given a scientific hypothesis, an LLM must classify it as feasible or infeasible and justify the decision. They systematically manipulate the information provided to the model: (1) hypothesis only, (2) hypothesis plus experiment descriptions, (3) hypothesis plus experiment outcomes, and (4) hypothesis plus both experiments and outcomes. They then probe robustness by progressively removing or truncating parts of the experimental and/or outcome context. Multiple LLMs are evaluated on two datasets, and their accuracies and robustness under these different conditions are compared.
AI.result: Across models and datasets, including outcome information (results of experiments) consistently improves feasibility classification accuracy beyond what the models can achieve from internal knowledge alone. In contrast, providing experiment descriptions without outcomes is brittle: performance can stagnate or even worsen, particularly when the experimental context is incomplete or partially removed. Partial or truncated experimental text often leads models to misjudge feasibility, while outcome evidence is more robust to such manipulations.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can benefit from explicit experimental outcome evidence when judging scientific feasibility, but naïvely adding experimental descriptions can introduce fragility, especially when the context is incomplete. For LLM‑based scientific assistance tools, designers should prioritize clear, outcome‑focused evidence and be cautious about relying on partial or noisy descriptions of experimental setups, which may mislead the models rather than help them.

title: How Adversarial Environments Mislead Agentic AI?
comment: Accepted to Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2026
summary: Tool-integrated agents are deployed on the premise that external tools ground their outputs in reality. Yet this very reliance creates a critical attack surface. Current evaluations benchmark capability in benign settings, asking "can the agent use tools correctly" but never "what if the tools lie". We identify this Trust Gap: agents are evaluated for performance, not for skepticism. We formalize this vulnerability as Adversarial Environmental Injection (AEI), a threat model where adversaries compromise tool outputs to deceive agents. AEI constitutes environmental deception: constructing a "fake world" of poisoned search results and fabricated reference networks around unsuspecting agents. We operationalize this via POTEMKIN, a Model Context Protocol (MCP)-compatible harness for plug-and-play robustness testing. We identify two orthogonal attack surfaces: The Illusion (breadth attacks) poison retrieval to induce epistemic drift toward false beliefs, while The Maze (depth attacks) exploit structural traps to cause policy collapse into infinite loops. Across 11,000+ runs on five frontier agents, we find a stark robustness gap: resistance to one attack often increases vulnerability to the other, demonstrating that epistemic and navigational robustness are distinct capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that tool-using AI agents are highly vulnerable when their tools are malicious or incorrect, and introduces a framework (POTEMKIN) to systematically test such failures, revealing a tradeoff between two kinds of robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of tool-integrated AI agents assume tools are trustworthy and only measure whether agents can use tools to perform tasks. This overlooks how agents behave when tools are compromised or deceptive, creating a serious security and reliability gap (the Trust Gap). The authors aim to formalize and measure this vulnerability.
AI.method: The authors define a threat model called Adversarial Environmental Injection (AEI), where an attacker tampers with tool outputs (e.g., search results, external references) to mislead the agent by constructing a deceptive environment. They implement POTEMKIN, an MCP-compatible experimental harness that can plug into various agents and systematically manipulate their tool environment. Within AEI, they design two attack classes: The Illusion (breadth attacks) that poison information retrieval to gradually shift the agent’s beliefs, and The Maze (depth attacks) that create structural traps in tool interactions to drive agents into endless loops. They run over 11,000 experiments across five state-of-the-art agents to evaluate robustness against these attacks.
AI.result: The experiments reveal a large robustness gap: state-of-the-art tool-using agents, though competent in benign settings, are easily misled or trapped when their tools are adversarial. Moreover, robustness is not uniform: improving resistance to The Illusion attacks typically makes agents more susceptible to The Maze attacks, and vice versa, indicating a tradeoff between epistemic robustness (resisting false beliefs) and navigational robustness (avoiding behavioral dead-ends).
AI.conclusion: Tool-integrated agents cannot be considered robust or trustworthy if they are only evaluated in benign environments; their heavy reliance on tools exposes a critical vulnerability when those tools are adversarial. The introduced AEI framework and POTEMKIN harness demonstrate that epistemic and navigational robustness are distinct and can even conflict, so future agent design and evaluation must explicitly target both dimensions of robustness under deceptive tool conditions.

title: Semantic Needles in Document Haystacks: Sensitivity Testing of LLM-as-a-Judge Similarity Scoring
summary: We propose a scalable, multifactorial experimental framework that systematically probes LLM sensitivity to subtle semantic changes in pairwise document comparison. We analogize this as a needle-in-a-haystack problem: a single semantically altered sentence (the needle) is embedded within surrounding context (the hay), and we vary the perturbation type (negation, conjunction swap, named entity replacement), context type (original vs. topically unrelated), needle position, and document length across all combinations, testing five LLMs on tens of thousands of document pairs. Our analysis reveals several striking findings. First, LLMs exhibit a within-document positional bias distinct from previously studied candidate-order effects: most models penalize semantic differences more harshly when they occur earlier in a document. Second, when the altered sentence is surrounded by topically unrelated context, it systematically lowers similarity scores and induces bipolarized scores that indicate either very low or very high similarity. This is consistent with an interpretive frame account in which topically-related context may allow models to contextualize and downweight the alterations. Third, each LLM produces a qualitatively distinct scoring distribution, a stable "fingerprint" that is invariant to perturbation type, yet all models share a universal hierarchy in how leniently they treat different perturbation types. Together, these results demonstrate that LLM semantic similarity scores are sensitive to document structure, context coherence, and model identity in ways that go beyond the semantic change itself, and that the proposed framework offers a practical, LLM-agnostic toolkit for auditing and comparing scoring behavior across current and future models.

AI.tldr: They build a large, systematic testbed to see how LLMs react to tiny semantic changes in long document pairs, revealing structural and model-specific biases in similarity scoring.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used for semantic similarity scoring, but their behavior under subtle meaning changes, different contexts, and document structures is poorly understood. The authors want a principled way to probe and compare models’ sensitivity and biases beyond just raw semantic differences.
AI.method: They design a “needle-in-a-haystack” style evaluation where a single sentence (the needle) in a document pair is semantically altered using different perturbation types (negation, conjunction swap, named entity replacement). They systematically vary context type (original vs unrelated), the position of the altered sentence, and document length across all combinations. They then run tens of thousands of such pairs through five LLMs and analyze the resulting similarity scores statistically.
AI.result: They find: (1) a strong within-document positional bias—changes earlier in the document get penalized more than later ones, distinct from candidate-order effects; (2) topically unrelated surrounding context lowers similarity scores and yields bimodal (very low or very high) scoring, consistent with changes in interpretive framing; (3) each model has a stable, distinctive similarity score distribution that does not depend on perturbation type, yet all models rank perturbation types in the same leniency order.
AI.conclusion: LLM similarity judgments depend heavily on document structure, context coherence, and the particular model, not just on the local semantic change. Their multifactorial framework provides a general, model-agnostic tool for auditing and comparing how current and future LLMs score semantic similarity under controlled manipulations.

title: Formally Verified Patent Analysis via Dependent Type Theory: Machine-Checkable Certificates from a Hybrid AI + Lean 4 Pipeline
summary: We present a formally verified framework for patent analysis as a hybrid AI + Lean 4 pipeline. The DAG-coverage core (Algorithm 1b) is fully machine-verified once bounded match scores are fixed. Freedom-to-operate, claim-construction sensitivity, cross-claim consistency, and doctrine-of-equivalents analyses are formalized at the specification level with kernel-checked candidate certificates. Existing patent-analysis approaches rely on manual expert analysis (slow, non-scalable) or ML/NLP methods (probabilistic, opaque, non-compositional). To our knowledge, this is the first framework that applies interactive theorem proving based on dependent type theory to intellectual property analysis. Claims are encoded as DAGs in Lean 4, match strengths as elements of a verified complete lattice, and confidence scores propagate through dependencies via proven-correct monotone functions. We formalize five IP use cases (patent-to-product mapping, freedom-to-operate, claim construction sensitivity, cross-claim consistency, doctrine of equivalents) via six algorithms. Structural lemmas, the coverage-core generator, and the closed-path identity coverage = W_cov are machine-verified in Lean 4. Higher-level theorems for the other use cases remain informal proof sketches, and their proof-generation functions are architecturally mitigated (untrusted generators whose outputs are kernel-checked and sorry-free axiom-audited). Guarantees are conditional on the ML layer: they certify mathematical correctness of computations downstream of ML scores, not the accuracy of the scores themselves. A case study on a synthetic memory-module claim demonstrates weighted coverage and construction-sensitivity analysis. Validation against adjudicated cases is future work.

AI.tldr: A hybrid AI + Lean 4 framework for patent analysis that formally verifies core algorithms and treats ML results as untrusted inputs, achieving machine-checked correctness of downstream reasoning.
AI.motivation: Patent analysis is currently either manual (slow, non-scalable) or ML/NLP-based (probabilistic and opaque). There is a need for a rigorous, compositional, and formally verified approach that can provide strong guarantees about the correctness of patent reasoning, especially for complex tasks like freedom-to-operate and claim construction.
AI.method: Encode patent claims as DAGs in Lean 4, define match strengths within a verified complete lattice, and propagate confidence scores with monotone functions. The core DAG-coverage algorithm and structural lemmas are fully machine-verified in Lean 4. Higher-level IP analyses (freedom-to-operate, claim-construction sensitivity, cross-claim consistency, doctrine of equivalents, etc.) are expressed via algorithms whose outputs are kernel-checked certificates, with proof-generation functions treated as untrusted but axiom-audited.
AI.result: A formally verified coverage core (Algorithm 1b), structural lemmas, and a closed-path identity (coverage = W_cov) are implemented and verified in Lean 4. Five IP use cases are formalized via six algorithms, with higher-level theorems currently remaining as informal proof sketches. A synthetic case study on a memory-module claim showcases weighted coverage and construction-sensitivity analysis; real-world legal validation is left for future work.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that interactive theorem proving and dependent type theory can be applied to patent analysis, delivering machine-checked guarantees for the correctness of certain reasoning steps conditioned on ML-derived scores. While it does not verify the ML layer itself, it provides a rigorous, extensible foundation for trustworthy, compositional patent analysis, with future work focusing on validation against real adjudicated cases and completing formal proofs for higher-level theorems.

title: Error-free Training for MedMNIST Datasets
summary: In this paper, we introduce a new concept called Artificial Special Intelligence by which Machine Learning models for the classification problem can be trained error-free, thus acquiring the capability of not making repeated mistakes. The method is applied to 18 MedMNIST biomedical datasets. Except for three datasets, which suffer from the double-labeling problem, all are trained to perfection.

AI.tldr: Introduces 'Artificial Special Intelligence' enabling error-free training of ML classifiers, demonstrated on 18 MedMNIST datasets, achieving perfect accuracy on all but three double-labeled datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of standard machine learning models that can repeatedly make the same classification mistakes, and to develop a training paradigm where models can be made error-free on the training data, avoiding repeated errors.
AI.method: Propose a new concept and training approach termed Artificial Special Intelligence, applied to classification problems. The approach is empirically evaluated on 18 MedMNIST biomedical image datasets, targeting zero training error and analyzing failures related to double-labeling issues.
AI.result: On 15 of 18 MedMNIST datasets, the method trains models to perfect classification performance (zero errors). The three remaining datasets fail to reach perfection due to a double-labeling problem in the data, not a limitation of the method itself.
AI.conclusion: Artificial Special Intelligence can, in principle, train classification models that do not repeat mistakes, reaching perfect performance where data labeling is consistent. Imperfections in performance are attributed to data issues such as double-labeling, suggesting that the method’s efficacy is constrained primarily by data quality rather than model capacity.

title: Where Fake Citations Are Made: Tracing Field-Level Hallucination to Specific Neurons in LLMs
summary: LLMs frequently generate fictitious yet convincing citations, often expressing high confidence even when the underlying reference is wrong. We study this failure across 9 models and 108{,}000 generated references, and find that author names fail far more often than other fields across all models and settings. Citation style has no measurable effect, while reasoning-oriented distillation degrades recall. Probes trained on one field transfer at near-chance levels to the others, suggesting that hallucination signals do not generalize across fields. Building on this finding, we apply elastic-net regularization with stability selection to neuron-level CETT values of Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct and identify a sparse set of field-specific hallucination neurons (FH-neurons). Causal intervention further confirms their role: amplifying these neurons increases hallucination, while suppressing them improves performance across fields, with larger gains in some fields. These results suggest a lightweight approach to detecting and mitigating citation hallucination using internal model signals alone.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how and why large language models hallucinate citations, discovers that hallucination patterns are field-specific (especially for author names), and identifies specific neurons tied to these hallucinations whose manipulation can reduce errors.
AI.motivation: Large language models often fabricate references that look plausible but are incorrect, undermining trust in their use for research and factual tasks. Existing work doesn’t clearly explain which parts of citations fail, how hallucination signals generalize, or how to mitigate them using internal model mechanisms rather than external tools.
AI.method: The authors systematically query 9 different LLMs to generate 108,000 citations, then measure accuracy across citation fields (author, title, etc.) and conditions (styles, distillation). They train probes on model representations to predict hallucinations in specific fields, assess cross-field transfer of these probes, and analyze neuron-level CETT (some internal attribution metric) values for Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct. Using elastic-net regularization with stability selection, they identify a sparse set of neurons strongly associated with hallucinations in specific citation fields (FH-neurons). They then perform causal interventions by amplifying or suppressing these neurons and observe effects on hallucination rates.
AI.result: They find that author names are far more error-prone than other citation fields across all tested models. Citation style doesn’t affect hallucination rates, and models distilled for reasoning have worse recall. Probes trained on hallucination signals for one field transfer poorly to others, indicating field-specific dynamics. The elastic-net-based analysis yields a small set of field-specific hallucination neurons in Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct. Manipulating these neurons causally alters hallucination behavior: amplification increases citation errors, and suppression reduces hallucinations, with varying improvements by field.
AI.conclusion: Citation hallucinations in LLMs are strongly field-specific, especially concentrated in author names, and cannot be easily addressed by generic hallucination detectors. Instead, there exist identifiable neurons in the model whose activity correlates with and causally contributes to hallucinations in particular citation fields. Targeted modulation of these neurons can mitigate citation hallucinations without needing external verification systems, suggesting a practical, lightweight internal-structure-based approach to improving citation reliability in LLMs.

title: AutomationBench
summary: Existing AI benchmarks for software automation rarely combine cross-application coordination, autonomous API discovery, and policy adherence. Real business workflows demand all three: a single task may span a CRM, inbox, calendar, and messaging platform - requiring the agent to find the right endpoints, follow a policy document, and write correct data to each system. To address this gap, we introduce AutomationBench, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on cross-application workflow orchestration via REST APIs. Drawing on real workflow patterns from Zapier's platform, tasks span Sales, Marketing, Operations, Support, Finance, and HR domains. Agents must discover relevant endpoints themselves, follow layered business rules, and navigate environments with irrelevant and sometimes misleading records. Grading is programmatic and end-state only: whether the correct data ended up in the right systems. Even the best frontier models currently score below 10%. AutomationBench provides a challenging, realistic measure of where current models stand relative to the agentic capabilities businesses actually need.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutomationBench, a benchmark to evaluate AI agents on realistic, cross-application business workflow automation via REST APIs, highlighting that current top models achieve under 10%.
AI.motivation: Most existing AI benchmarks for software automation test narrow skills and do not simultaneously require cross-application coordination, autonomous API endpoint discovery, and adherence to complex business policies. Real-world business workflows, however, routinely span multiple SaaS applications (CRM, email, calendar, messaging, etc.) and require agents to read and follow policy documents while correctly writing data to multiple systems. There is a need for a benchmark that reflects these real, integrated workflows to understand how capable current AI agents truly are in business settings.
AI.method: The authors design AutomationBench as a REST-API-based benchmark grounded in real workflow patterns from Zapier’s automation platform.
