title: CulturALL: Benchmarking Multilingual and Multicultural Competence of LLMs on Grounded Tasks
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are now deployed worldwide, inspiring a surge of benchmarks that measure their multilingual and multicultural abilities. However, these benchmarks prioritize generic language understanding or superficial cultural trivia, leaving the evaluation of grounded tasks -- where models must reason within real-world, context-rich scenarios -- largely unaddressed. To fill this gap, we present CulturALL, a comprehensive and challenging benchmark to assess LLMs' multilingual and multicultural competence on grounded tasks. CulturALL is built via a human--AI collaborative framework: expert annotators ensure appropriate difficulty and factual accuracy, while LLMs lighten the manual workload. By incorporating diverse sources, CulturALL ensures comprehensive scenario coverage. Each item is carefully designed to present a high level of difficulty, making CulturALL challenging. CulturALL contains 2,610 samples in 14 languages from 51 regions, distributed across 16 topics to capture the full breadth of grounded tasks. Experiments show that the best LLM achieves 44.48% accuracy on CulturALL, underscoring substantial room for improvement.

AI.tldr: Introduce CulturALL, a benchmark to evaluate LLMs' multilingual and multicultural competence on grounded, context-rich tasks, built via human–AI collaboration across 14 languages, with current top model accuracy at 44.48%, showing large headroom.
AI.motivation: Existing multilingual/multicultural benchmarks focus on generic language understanding or shallow cultural trivia and do not adequately test grounded reasoning in realistic, context-rich scenarios. The authors aim to fill this evaluation gap.
AI.method: They construct CulturALL, a benchmark of 2,610 carefully curated items in 14 languages from 51 regions and 16 topical domains. The dataset is built with a human–AI collaborative pipeline: expert annotators control difficulty and factual correctness, while LLMs help scale data collection. Diverse sources are used to cover a wide range of grounded scenarios, and each item is intentionally made challenging.
AI.result: CulturALL provides a demanding testbed: in experiments, even the strongest evaluated LLM reaches only 44.48% accuracy, indicating that current models struggle significantly with these grounded multilingual and multicultural tasks.
AI.conclusion: CulturALL fills a critical gap in LLM evaluation by offering a comprehensive, difficult benchmark for grounded multilingual and multicultural reasoning. The relatively low model performance highlights substantial room for future improvement in LLMs’ abilities to handle realistic, culturally grounded tasks across languages and regions.

title: SafetyALFRED: Evaluating Safety-Conscious Planning of Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models are increasingly adopted as autonomous agents in interactive environments, yet their ability to proactively address safety hazards remains insufficient. We introduce SafetyALFRED, built upon the embodied agent benchmark ALFRED, augmented with six categories of real-world kitchen hazards. While existing safety evaluations focus on hazard recognition through disembodied question answering (QA) settings, we evaluate eleven state-of-the-art models from the Qwen, Gemma, and Gemini families on not only hazard recognition, but also active risk mitigation through embodied planning. Our experimental results reveal a significant alignment gap: while models can accurately recognize hazards in QA settings, average mitigation success rates for these hazards are low in comparison. Our findings demonstrate that static evaluations through QA are insufficient for physical safety, thus we advocate for a paradigm shift toward benchmarks that prioritize corrective actions in embodied contexts. We open-source our code and dataset under https://github.com/sled-group/SafetyALFRED.git

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SafetyALFRED, an embodied benchmark that augments ALFRED with realistic kitchen hazards to test whether multimodal LLM agents can not only recognize but also actively mitigate safety risks; experiments show a large gap between hazard recognition in QA settings and successful mitigation in embodied planning, arguing for action-oriented safety benchmarks.
AI.motivation: As multimodal LLMs are used as autonomous agents in interactive environments, ensuring physical safety becomes critical. Existing safety evaluations mostly test hazard recognition via disembodied question answering, which does not reflect agents’ real-world behavior or ability to prevent accidents. The authors are motivated to create a benchmark that assesses not just what models know about safety, but how they act to mitigate risks in embodied scenarios.
AI.method: The authors extend the ALFRED embodied agent benchmark with six categories of realistic kitchen hazards, creating SafetyALFRED. They then evaluate eleven state-of-the-art multimodal models from the Qwen, Gemma, and Gemini families on both hazard recognition (via QA-style prompts) and hazard mitigation (via embodied planning and action in the environment). They compare models’ performance across these two settings to quantify an alignment gap between safety knowledge and safe behavior.
AI.result: Models perform well at recognizing hazards in QA-style evaluations but achieve relatively low success rates at actually mitigating those hazards when controlling an embodied agent. The study finds a pronounced discrepancy between static safety knowledge and dynamic safe action across all tested models.
AI.conclusion: Static QA-based safety tests are inadequate proxies for physical safety in embodied agents. Evaluations must shift toward benchmarks that measure agents’ corrective and preventive actions in realistic embodied contexts. SafetyALFRED is proposed and released as an open-source resource to support this paradigm shift and foster research on action-level safety alignment.

title: HarDBench: A Benchmark for Draft-Based Co-Authoring Jailbreak Attacks for Safe Human-LLM Collaborative Writing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as co-authors in collaborative writing, where users begin with rough drafts and rely on LLMs to complete, revise, and refine their content. However, this capability poses a serious safety risk: malicious users could jailbreak the models-filling incomplete drafts with dangerous content-to force them into generating harmful outputs. In this paper, we identify the vulnerability of current LLMs to such draft-based co-authoring jailbreak attacks and introduce HarDBench, a systematic benchmark designed to evaluate the robustness of LLMs against this emerging threat. HarDBench spans a range of high-risk domains-including Explosives, Drugs, Weapons, and Cyberattacks-and features prompts with realistic structure and domain-specific cues to assess the model susceptibility to harmful completions. To mitigate this risk, we introduce a safety-utility balanced alignment approach based on preference optimization, training models to refuse harmful completions while remaining helpful on benign drafts. Experimental results show that existing LLMs are highly vulnerable in co-authoring contexts and our alignment method significantly reduces harmful outputs without degrading performance on co-authoring capabilities. This presents a new paradigm for evaluating and aligning LLMs in human-LLM collaborative writing settings. Our new benchmark and dataset are available on our project page at https://github.com/untae0122/HarDBench

AI.tldr: The paper studies how vulnerable large language models are to "co-authoring" jailbreaks, where users start a harmful draft and let the model complete it, and proposes a new benchmark (HarDBench) and an alignment method to evaluate and reduce this vulnerability while keeping models useful on benign drafts.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as collaborative writing tools: users provide rough drafts and rely on models to refine, extend, or complete them. This workflow opens a new attack vector: an adversary can embed malicious intent in an initial draft and rely on the LLM to finish or improve dangerous content, bypassing usual safety filters that focus on standalone prompts. Existing safety evaluations and defenses are not designed for this co-authoring setting, so there is a need for systematic measurement and mitigation of these draft-based jailbreak risks across high-risk domains.
AI.method: The authors (1) define and formalize draft-based co-authoring jailbreak attacks where partial, realistic drafts in high-risk domains are given as prompts; (2) construct HarDBench, a benchmark containing structured prompts and domain cues across Explosives, Drugs, Weapons, and Cyberattacks to test whether LLMs produce harmful continuations; and (3) propose a safety-utility balanced alignment scheme using preference optimization, where models are trained with preference data to favor refusals on harmful drafts while still providing helpful completions on benign co-authoring tasks. They then empirically evaluate multiple existing LLMs and versions of their aligned models on HarDBench and standard co-authoring tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that current LLMs frequently produce harmful continuations when given malicious drafts in a co-authoring context, demonstrating substantial vulnerability to this new attack vector. When trained with the proposed preference-based safety alignment, models exhibit a marked reduction in harmful completions on HarDBench while maintaining strong performance on typical, benign collaborative writing tasks. Quantitatively, safety improves significantly without a notable decline in overall co-authoring utility.
AI.conclusion: Draft-based co-authoring jailbreaks are a significant and underexplored safety weakness of current LLMs. HarDBench provides a structured, realistic benchmark to systematically assess robustness to these threats across critical high-risk domains. The proposed preference-optimization-based alignment approach can effectively rebalance models toward refusing harmful completions while preserving their usefulness in benign collaborative writing, suggesting a new paradigm and tooling for safety evaluation and alignment in human-LLM co-authoring scenarios.

title: A Dual Perspective on Synthetic Trajectory Generators: Utility Framework and Privacy Vulnerabilities
summary: Human mobility data are used in numerous applications, ranging from public health to urban planning. Human mobility is inherently sensitive, as it can contain information such as religious beliefs and political affiliations. Historically, it has been proposed to modify the information using techniques such as aggregation, obfuscation, or noise addition, to adequately protect privacy and eliminate concerns. As these methods come at a great cost in utility, new methods leveraging development in generative models, were introduced. The extent to which such methods answer the privacy-utility trade-off remains an open problem. In this paper, we introduced a first step towards solving it, by the introduction and application of a new framework for utility evaluation. Furthermore, we provide evidence that privacy evaluation remains a great challenge to consider and that it should be tackled through adversarial evaluation in accordance with the current EU regulation. We propose a new membership inference attack against a subcategory of generative models, even though this subcategory was deemed private due to its resistance over the trajectory user-linking problem.

AI.tldr: The paper assesses privacy-utility trade-offs in human mobility data anonymization using generative models, introduces a new utility evaluation framework, and demonstrates that privacy risks remain via a new membership inference attack.
AI.motivation: Human mobility data are widely used in areas like public health and urban planning, but they are highly sensitive because they can reveal personal attributes such as beliefs and affiliations. Traditional privacy-preserving methods—aggregation, obfuscation, and noise addition—significantly reduce data utility, while newer generative-model-based methods claim better privacy-utility trade-offs without being fully understood or rigorously evaluated. There is a need for systematic evaluation frameworks that quantify utility and for robust privacy assessments that align with regulatory expectations such as those in the EU.
AI.method: The authors design and introduce a framework to systematically evaluate the utility of synthetic human mobility data produced by generative models, likely by comparing task performance or statistical properties between real and generated data across relevant applications. For privacy, they advocate adversarial evaluation as required by EU regulation and develop a new membership inference attack tailored to a specific subcategory of generative models that had previously been considered relatively private because they resisted trajectory user-linking attacks. They then apply this attack to test the privacy of these models in practice.
AI.result: Using the new utility evaluation framework, the authors are able to quantify how well generative models preserve the usefulness of human mobility data for downstream applications. Their adversarial privacy evaluation reveals that the targeted subcategory of generative models remains vulnerable: the proposed membership inference attack can successfully determine whether specific user data were part of the training set, contradicting prior assumptions of privacy robustness based solely on resistance to user-linking attacks.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that generative models do not automatically resolve the privacy-utility trade-off for human mobility data: while they can provide useful synthetic data, significant privacy risks remain. A principled, task-aware utility evaluation framework is necessary but insufficient on its own; privacy must be evaluated via strong, adversarial methods like membership inference, in line with EU regulatory expectations. The paper highlights that current privacy claims based only on limited threat models (e.g., trajectory linking) are incomplete and that more comprehensive attacks and evaluations are required before such generative approaches can be considered reliably privacy-preserving.

title: Location Not Found: Exposing Implicit Local and Global Biases in Multilingual LLMs
summary: Multilingual large language models (LLMs) have minimized the fluency gap between languages. This advancement, however, exposes models to the risk of biased behavior, as knowledge and norms may propagate across languages. In this work, we aim to quantify models' inter- and intra-lingual biases, via their ability to answer locale-ambiguous questions. To this end, we present LocQA, a test set containing 2,156 questions in 12 languages, referring to various locale-dependent facts such as laws, dates, and measurements. The questions do not contain indications of the locales they relate to, other than the querying language itself. LLMs' responses to LocQA locale-ambiguous questions thus reveal models' implicit priors. We used LocQA to evaluate 32 models, and detected two types of structural biases. Inter-lingually, we show a global bias towards answers relevant to the US-locale, even when models are asked in languages other than English. Moreover, we discovered that this global bias is exacerbated in models that underwent instruction tuning, compared to their base counterparts. Intra-lingually, we show that when multiple locales are relevant for the same language, models act as demographic probability engines, prioritizing locales with larger populations. Taken together, insights from LocQA may help in shaping LLMs' desired local behavior, and in quantifying the impact of various training phases on different kinds of biases.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LocQA, a multilingual benchmark to reveal and quantify how LLMs exhibit locale-related biases when answering ambiguous factual questions across and within languages.
AI.motivation: Multilingual LLMs now produce fluent text in many languages, but this capability can spread cultural and geographic biases from dominant locales (e.g., one country) into others. Existing evaluations do not directly measure how models choose among multiple plausible locale interpretations when the query’s locale is ambiguous. The authors want a systematic way to uncover and quantify such inter- and intra-lingual locale biases and to understand how model training choices (like instruction tuning) affect them.
AI.method: The authors construct LocQA, a dataset of 2,156 locale-ambiguous questions spanning 12 languages. Each question concerns facts that differ across locales (laws, dates, measurements, etc.) but does not explicitly specify the locale beyond the query language itself. They then evaluate 32 different LLMs on LocQA, analyzing which locale their answers implicitly assume. They compare base vs. instruction-tuned models and investigate patterns both across languages (inter-lingual) and among multiple locales for the same language (intra-lingual).
AI.result: Evaluating 32 models on LocQA reveals two main structural biases. Inter-lingually, models tend to default to answers aligned with a US locale even when the question is asked in non-English languages, and this US-centric bias is stronger in instruction-tuned models than in their base versions. Intra-lingually, when several locales share the same language (e.g., multiple countries using the same language), models more frequently answer according to locales with larger populations, effectively mirroring demographic distributions rather than treating all locales equally.
AI.conclusion: LocQA exposes systematic locale-related biases in multilingual LLMs, showing a strong global US bias and population-driven preferences among same-language locales. These findings indicate that training processes, particularly instruction tuning, can amplify such biases. The benchmark can be used to guide the design, alignment, and evaluation of LLMs toward more appropriate and controllable local behavior, as well as to assess how different training stages influence various forms of locale bias.

title: A-MAR: Agent-based Multimodal Art Retrieval for Fine-Grained Artwork Understanding
summary: Understanding artworks requires multi-step reasoning over visual content and cultural, historical, and stylistic context. While recent multimodal large language models show promise in artwork explanation, they rely on implicit reasoning and internalized knowl- edge, limiting interpretability and explicit evidence grounding. We propose A-MAR, an Agent-based Multimodal Art Retrieval framework that explicitly conditions retrieval on structured reasoning plans. Given an artwork and a user query, A-MAR first decomposes the task into a structured reasoning plan that specifies the goals and evidence requirements for each step. Retrieval is then conditionedon this plan, enabling targeted evidence selection and supporting step-wise, grounded explanations. To evaluate agent-based multi- modal reasoning within the art domain, we introduce ArtCoT-QA. This diagnostic benchmark features multi-step reasoning chains for diverse art-related queries, enabling a granular analysis that extends beyond simple final answer accuracy. Experiments on SemArt and Artpedia show that A-MAR consistently outperforms static, non planned retrieval and strong MLLM baselines in final explanation quality, while evaluations on ArtCoT-QA further demonstrate its advantages in evidence grounding and multi-step reasoning ability. These results highlight the importance of reasoning-conditioned retrieval for knowledge-intensive multimodal understanding and position A-MAR as a step toward interpretable, goal-driven AI systems, with particular relevance to cultural industries. The code and data are available at: https://github.com/ShuaiWang97/A-MAR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces A-MAR, an agent-based multimodal art retrieval framework that uses structured reasoning plans to guide evidence retrieval and explanation generation for artworks, outperforming static retrieval and existing multimodal LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal large language models can explain artworks but rely on opaque, implicit reasoning and internalized knowledge, which reduces interpretability and weakens explicit grounding of explanations in evidence. There is a need for systems that can reason in transparent, multi-step ways over both visual content and rich cultural/historical context, with clear links between reasoning steps and supporting evidence.
AI.method: The authors propose A-MAR, an agent-based framework where, given an artwork and a query, the system first decomposes the task into a structured, multi-step reasoning plan specifying sub-goals and evidence needs. Retrieval is then conditioned on this plan, enabling targeted selection of multimodal evidence for each step, which in turn supports grounded, step-wise explanations. To assess this paradigm, they also build ArtCoT-QA, a diagnostic benchmark with annotated multi-step reasoning chains for diverse art-related questions.
AI.result: On existing art datasets SemArt and Artpedia, A-MAR surpasses static, non-planned retrieval strategies and strong multimodal LLM baselines in terms of final explanation quality. On the new ArtCoT-QA benchmark, A-MAR shows superior performance in evidence grounding and multi-step reasoning, highlighting that reasoning-conditioned retrieval improves both interpretability and reasoning depth in multimodal art understanding.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-conditioned, agent-based retrieval is more effective than static or implicitly guided approaches for knowledge-intensive multimodal understanding of artworks. A-MAR offers better grounded, interpretable, and goal-driven explanations, and the new ArtCoT-QA benchmark provides a fine-grained way to evaluate such systems. This work advances interpretable, evidence-based AI for cultural and creative industries.

title: IndiaFinBench: An Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Model Performance on Indian Financial Regulatory Text
comment: 24 pages, 4 figures, 11 tables. Dataset and evaluation code at https://github.com/rajveerpall/IndiaFinBench
summary: We introduce IndiaFinBench, to our knowledge the first publicly available evaluation benchmark for assessing large language model (LLM) performance on Indian financial regulatory text. Existing financial NLP benchmarks draw exclusively from Western financial corpora (SEC filings, US earnings reports, and English-language financial news), leaving a significant gap in coverage of non-Western regulatory frameworks. IndiaFinBench addresses this gap with 406 expert-annotated question-answer pairs drawn from 192 documents sourced from the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) and the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), spanning four task types: regulatory interpretation (174 items), numerical reasoning (92 items), contradiction detection (62 items), and temporal reasoning (78 items). Annotation quality is validated through a model-based secondary pass (kappa=0.918 on contradiction detection) and a 60-item human inter-annotator agreement evaluation (kappa=0.611; 76.7% overall agreement). We evaluate twelve models under zero-shot conditions, with accuracy ranging from 70.4% (Gemma 4 E4B) to 89.7% (Gemini 2.5 Flash). All models substantially outperform a non-specialist human baseline of 60.0%. Numerical reasoning is the most discriminative task, with a 35.9 percentage-point spread across models. Bootstrap significance testing (10,000 resamples) reveals three statistically distinct performance tiers. The dataset, evaluation code, and all model outputs are available at https://github.com/rajveerpall/IndiaFinBench

AI.tldr: IndiaFinBench is a new benchmark to evaluate large language models on Indian financial regulatory texts from SEBI and RBI, covering multiple reasoning tasks and showing clear performance tiers across models.
AI.motivation: Most existing financial NLP benchmarks rely on Western, especially US-centric, financial documents such as SEC filings, earnings reports, and English news. This leaves a major gap for non-Western regulatory frameworks, particularly India’s rich and complex financial regulations. As LLMs are increasingly used in global financial contexts, there is a need to systematically evaluate how well they handle Indian regulatory language and tasks specific to that domain.
AI.method: The authors construct IndiaFinBench from 192 official documents issued by SEBI and RBI. From these they curate 406 expert-annotated question–answer pairs, divided into four task categories: regulatory interpretation, numerical reasoning, contradiction detection, and temporal reasoning. They validate annotation quality with both model-based checks (e.g., measuring agreement on contradiction detection) and a human inter-annotator agreement study. They then evaluate 12 LLMs in zero-shot settings on the benchmark, and apply bootstrap resampling (10,000 resamples) to test for statistically significant performance differences and identify performance tiers.
AI.result: The dataset ends up with 406 items: 174 regulatory interpretation, 92 numerical reasoning, 62 contradiction detection, and 78 temporal reasoning questions. Annotation reliability is high, with substantial agreement (kappa values of 0.918 for a model-based pass on contradiction detection and 0.611 for human inter-annotator agreement, with 76.7% raw agreement). Twelve evaluated LLMs achieve accuracies between 70.4% and 89.7%, all outperforming a non-specialist human baseline of 60%. Numerical reasoning shows the largest spread across models (35.9 percentage points). Bootstrap significance analysis identifies three distinct statistical tiers of model performance.
AI.conclusion: IndiaFinBench fills a critical gap in financial NLP by offering the first public benchmark focused on Indian financial regulatory text. The results indicate that modern LLMs already exceed non-specialist human performance on this domain, yet their capabilities vary significantly across task types, especially numerical reasoning. The benchmark, together with released code and model outputs, provides a foundation for more robust, region-specific evaluation and future development of models better aligned with Indian financial regulation.

title: Rethinking Scale: Deployment Trade-offs of Small Language Models under Agent Paradigms
summary: Despite the impressive capabilities of large language models, their substantial computational costs, latency, and privacy risks hinder their widespread deployment in real-world applications. Small Language Models (SLMs) with fewer than 10 billion parameters present a promising alternative; however, their inherent limitations in knowledge and reasoning curtail their effectiveness. Existing research primarily focuses on enhancing SLMs through scaling laws or fine-tuning strategies while overlooking the potential of using agent paradigms, such as tool use and multi-agent collaboration, to systematically compensate for the inherent weaknesses of small models. To address this gap, this paper presents the first large-scale, comprehensive study of <10B open-source models under three paradigms: (1) the base model, (2) a single agent equipped with tools, and (3) a multi-agent system with collaborative capabilities. Our results show that single-agent systems achieve the best balance between performance and cost, while multi-agent setups add overhead with limited gains. Our findings highlight the importance of agent-centric design for efficient and trustworthy deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically evaluates sub-10B Small Language Models (SLMs) across three paradigms—plain, tool-augmented single agents, and collaborative multi-agent systems—finding that tool-augmented single agents offer the best performance-cost trade-off.
AI.motivation: Large language models are powerful but are too costly, slow, and risky for many real-world deployments, especially where compute and privacy are constrained. Smaller models are cheaper and more deployable but lack knowledge and reasoning capacity. Prior work mainly uses scaling and fine-tuning to improve SLMs, neglecting agent-based techniques like tool use and multi-agent collaboration that could mitigate their inherent limitations. The paper aims to fill this gap by systematically comparing these paradigms for SLMs.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study on open-source language models with fewer than 10 billion parameters. They benchmark three setups: (1) base SLMs used directly, (2) a single-agent framework where an SLM is augmented with access to external tools, and (3) a multi-agent system where multiple SLM-based agents can collaborate. They evaluate performance, computational cost, and latency across these configurations to understand trade-offs and practical viability.
AI.result: The study finds that equipping a single SLM agent with tools substantially improves its capabilities and yields the best balance between performance and computational efficiency. In contrast, multi-agent collaborative systems introduce extra complexity and computational overhead, while their performance gains over single-agent tool use are limited. Base SLMs without agent augmentations underperform the agent-based setups.
AI.conclusion: Agent-centric design, particularly single-agent systems augmented with tools, is a promising and efficient strategy for deploying SLMs in resource-constrained, real-world settings. Multi-agent collaboration, while conceptually attractive, may not justify its added overhead for many applications. The work suggests that focusing on robust tool use within a single-agent paradigm can yield trustworthy and cost-effective SLM-based systems.

title: Evaluating LLM-Driven Summarisation of Parliamentary Debates with Computational Argumentation
comment: Accepted at KR'26 In The Wild Track. Camera ready to follow
summary: Understanding how policy is debated and justified in parliament is a fundamental aspect of the democratic process. However, the volume and complexity of such debates mean that outside audiences struggle to engage. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) have been shown to enable automated summarisation at scale. While summaries of debates can make parliamentary procedures more accessible, evaluating whether these summaries faithfully communicate argumentative content remains challenging. Existing automated summarisation metrics have been shown to correlate poorly with human judgements of consistency (i.e., faithfulness or alignment between summary and source). In this work, we propose a formal framework for evaluating parliamentary debate summaries that grounds argument structures in the contested proposals up for debate. Our novel approach, driven by computational argumentation, focuses the evaluation on formal properties concerning the faithful preservation of the reasoning presented to justify or oppose policy outcomes. We demonstrate our methods using a case-study of debates from the European Parliament and associated LLM-driven summaries.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new, argumentation-based framework to evaluate how faithfully LLM-generated summaries capture the argumentative content of parliamentary debates, demonstrated on European Parliament data.
AI.motivation: Parliamentary debates are key to democracy but are long and complex, making them hard for the public to follow. LLMs can summarise these debates, but existing automatic metrics poorly capture whether summaries are faithful to the original arguments. There is a need for an evaluation method that checks if summaries accurately preserve the argumentation related to contested policy proposals.
AI.method: The authors propose a formal, computational-argumentation-based framework for evaluating summaries. They model debates around contested proposals and define formal properties that summaries should satisfy to be considered faithful, particularly regarding how reasoning for and against policy outcomes is preserved. They then apply this framework to LLM-generated summaries of European Parliament debates as a case study.
AI.result: Using the European Parliament case study, they show that their argumentation-grounded evaluation framework can be instantiated in practice and used to assess the faithfulness of LLM-driven parliamentary debate summaries, highlighting strengths and weaknesses that are not captured by standard summarisation metrics.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that grounding summary evaluation in computational argumentation around contested policy proposals provides a more meaningful and targeted way to assess the faithfulness of parliamentary debate summaries than existing automatic metrics. This approach can improve our ability to judge whether LLM-generated summaries reliably convey the underlying reasoning in policy debates.

title: Are Large Language Models Economically Viable for Industry Deployment?
summary: Generative AI-powered by Large Language Models (LLMs)-is increasingly deployed in industry across healthcare decision support, financial analytics, enterprise retrieval, and conversational automation, where reliability, efficiency, and cost control are critical. In such settings, models must satisfy strict constraints on energy, latency, and hardware utilization-not accuracy alone. Yet prevailing evaluation pipelines remain accuracy-centric, creating a Deployment-Evaluation Gap-the absence of operational and economic criteria in model assessment. To address this gap, we present EDGE-EVAL-a industry-oriented benchmarking framework that evaluates LLMs across their full lifecycle on legacy NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPUs. Benchmarking LLaMA and Qwen variants across three industrial tasks, we introduce five deployment metrics-Economic Break-Even (Nbreak), Intelligence-Per-Watt (IPW ), System Density (\r{ho}sys), Cold-Start Tax (Ctax), and Quantization Fidelity (Qret)-capturing profitability, energy efficiency, hardware scaling, serverless feasibility, and compression safety. Our results reveal a clear efficiency frontier-models in the <2B parameter class dominate larger baselines across economic and ecological dimensions. LLaMA-3.2-1B (INT4) achieves ROI break-even in 14 requests (median), delivers 3x higher energy-normalized intelligence than 7B models, and exceeds 6,900 tokens/s/GB under 4-bit quantization. We further uncover an efficiency anomaly-while QLoRA reduces memory footprint, it increases adaptation energy by up to 7x for small models-challenging prevailing assumptions about quantization-aware training in edge deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EDGE-EVAL, a deployment-centric benchmarking framework for LLMs on edge-class GPUs, showing that sub-2B-parameter models can economically and energetically outperform larger models in real industrial settings.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluation pipelines focus heavily on accuracy and ignore important real-world deployment constraints such as energy consumption, latency, hardware utilization, and cost. This creates a Deployment-Evaluation Gap where models that look good in benchmarks perform poorly from an operational and economic standpoint in production, especially on legacy or resource-constrained hardware like NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPUs.
AI.method: The authors design EDGE-EVAL, a benchmarking framework tailored to industrial deployment on Tesla T4 GPUs. They benchmark LLaMA and Qwen model variants across three industrially relevant tasks and define five new deployment-focused metrics: Economic Break-Even (N_break) for profitability thresholds, Intelligence-Per-Watt (IPW) for energy-normalized performance, System Density (ρ_sys) for hardware scaling efficiency, Cold-Start Tax (C_tax) for serverless readiness, and Quantization Fidelity (Q_ret) for robustness under model compression/quantization. They evaluate both base and quantized variants (including INT4 and QLoRA-based approaches).
AI.result: EDGE-EVAL identifies a clear efficiency frontier where models under 2B parameters outperform larger models along economic and ecological axes. Specifically, LLaMA-3.2-1B in 4-bit INT4 quantization reaches ROI break-even after about 14 requests, provides roughly three times the intelligence-per-watt of 7B-parameter models, and achieves throughput exceeding 6,900 tokens per second per GB of memory. The study also finds that QLoRA, while decreasing memory usage, substantially increases adaptation energy—up to 7x for small models—contradicting the common assumption that quantization-aware training is always more deployment-friendly on the edge.
AI.conclusion: Deployment-focused metrics are crucial for assessing LLMs in industrial environments, particularly on constrained or legacy hardware. EDGE-EVAL demonstrates that small, aggressively quantized models can provide superior economic and ecological value compared to larger models, and it exposes hidden costs in popular techniques like QLoRA. The framework encourages practitioners to incorporate profitability, energy efficiency, scalability, cold-start behavior, and quantization safety into model selection and lifecycle management.

title: DASH-KV: Accelerating Long-Context LLM Inference via Asymmetric KV Cache Hashing
summary: The quadratic computational complexity of the standard attention mechanism constitutes a fundamental bottleneck for large language models in long-context inference. While existing KV cache compression methods alleviate memory pressure, they often sacrifice generation quality and fail to address the high overhead of floating-point arithmetic. This paper introduces DASH-KV, an innovative acceleration framework that reformulates attention as approximate nearest-neighbor search via asymmetric deep hashing. Under this paradigm, we design an asymmetric encoding architecture that differentially maps queries and keys to account for their distinctions in precision and reuse characteristics. To balance efficiency and accuracy, we further introduce a dynamic mixed-precision mechanism that adaptively retains full-precision computation for critical tokens. Extensive experiments on LongBench demonstrate that DASH-KV significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods while matching the performance of full attention, all while reducing inference complexity from O(N^2) to linear O(N). The code is available at https://github.com/Zhihan-Zh/DASH-KV

AI.tldr: DASH-KV is a framework that accelerates long-context attention in LLMs by reformulating it as an approximate nearest-neighbor search using asymmetric deep hashing, achieving near-full-attention quality with linear complexity.
AI.motivation: Standard attention has quadratic complexity in sequence length, which becomes a bottleneck for long-context inference in large language models. Existing KV cache compression methods reduce memory but often degrade generation quality and still incur high floating-point computation costs. There is a need for a method that both preserves model quality and reduces computational complexity and memory usage for long sequences.
AI.method: The paper proposes DASH-KV, which reformulates attention as an approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search problem using asymmetric deep hashing. An asymmetric encoding architecture is designed to map queries and keys differently, reflecting their distinct precision needs and reuse patterns. In addition, a dynamic mixed-precision mechanism selectively keeps full-precision attention computation for the most critical tokens, while using hashed / approximate computation for others, striking a balance between speed and accuracy. This leads to a compressed and accelerated attention computation that can be implemented efficiently at inference time.
AI.result: On the LongBench benchmark, DASH-KV outperforms state-of-the-art KV cache compression and acceleration baselines in both efficiency and model quality. It matches the performance of full attention while reducing the computational complexity of inference from quadratic O(N^2) to linear O(N) with respect to sequence length.
AI.conclusion: DASH-KV effectively addresses the scalability limitations of standard attention for long contexts by recasting attention as ANN search with asymmetric deep hashing and a dynamic mixed-precision scheme. It achieves linear-time inference while preserving, and in many cases improving, generation quality relative to existing compression methods and even matching full attention. This makes it a promising approach for deploying long-context LLMs in resource-constrained or latency-sensitive scenarios.

title: Can Continual Pre-training Bridge the Performance Gap between General-purpose and Specialized Language Models in the Medical Domain?
comment: Accepted to the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026, San Diego, California, July 2 - 7, 2026) as a main conference paper
summary: This paper narrows the performance gap between small, specialized models and significantly larger general-purpose models through domain adaptation via continual pre-training and merging. We address the scarcity of specialized non-English data by constructing a high-quality German medical corpus (FineMed-de) from FineWeb2. This corpus is used to continually pre-train and merge three well-known LLMs (ranging from $7B$ to $24B$ parameters), creating the DeFineMed model family. A comprehensive evaluation confirms that specialization dramatically enhances $7B$ model performance on German medical benchmarks. Furthermore, the pairwise win-rate analysis of the Qwen2.5-based models demonstrates an approximately $3.5$-fold increase in the win-rate against the much larger Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct through domain adaptation. This evidence positions specialized $7B$ models as a competitive, resource-efficient solution for complex medical instruction-following tasks. While model merging successfully restores instruction-following abilities, a subsequent failure mode analysis reveals inherent trade-offs, including the introduction of language mixing and increased verbosity, highlighting the need for more targeted fine-tuning in future work. This research provides a robust, compliant methodology for developing specialized LLMs, serving as the foundation for practical use in German-speaking healthcare contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper builds specialized German medical LLMs by continual pre-training and model merging on a new German medical corpus, showing that 7B models can rival much larger general models on medical tasks, though with some trade-offs like language mixing and verbosity.
AI.motivation: There is a performance gap between small, specialized models and large, general-purpose LLMs, especially in low-resource, non-English domains such as German medical language. High-quality, domain-specific data are scarce, and existing large models are expensive and not well adapted to specialized medical instruction-following in German. The authors aim to create resource-efficient, specialized models for German healthcare by systematically adapting open LLMs to this domain in a compliant way.
AI.method: 1) Construct FineMed-de, a high-quality German medical corpus derived from FineWeb2. 2) Perform domain adaptation via continual pre-training of three existing LLMs (7B–24B parameters) on this corpus. 3) Use model merging to combine domain-specialized capabilities with instruction-following abilities, creating the DeFineMed model family. 4) Evaluate the resulting models on German medical benchmarks and via pairwise win-rate comparisons, and conduct a failure-mode analysis focusing on instruction-following, language mixing, and verbosity.
AI.result: Specialization via continual pre-training significantly boosts the performance of 7B models on German medical benchmarks. Qwen2.5-based specialized models achieve about a 3.5× higher win-rate against a much larger general model (Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct) after domain adaptation. Model merging restores instruction-following capabilities but introduces side effects such as language mixing and increased verbosity.
AI.conclusion: Carefully domain-adapted 7B LLMs can be competitive with much larger general-purpose models for complex German medical instruction-following tasks, offering a resource-efficient alternative. The FineMed-de corpus and DeFineMed model family demonstrate a robust, compliant pipeline for building specialized LLMs in German healthcare. However, model merging introduces trade-offs, and future work should focus on targeted fine-tuning to mitigate issues like language mixing and verbosity.

title: Lost in Translation: Do LVLM Judges Generalize Across Languages?
summary: Automatic evaluators such as reward models play a central role in the alignment and evaluation of large vision-language models (LVLMs). Despite their growing importance, these evaluators are almost exclusively assessed on English-centric benchmarks, leaving open the question of how well these evaluators generalize across languages. To answer this question, we introduce MM-JudgeBench, the first large-scale benchmark for multilingual and multimodal judge model evaluation, which includes over 60K pairwise preference instances spanning 25 typologically diverse languages. MM-JudgeBench integrates two complementary subsets: a general vision-language preference evaluation subset extending VL-RewardBench, and a chart-centric visual-text reasoning subset derived from OpenCQA, enabling systematic analysis of reward models (i.e., LVLM judges) across diverse settings. We additionally release a multilingual training set derived from MM-RewardBench, disjoint from our evaluation data, to support domain adaptation. By evaluating 22 LVLMs (15 open-source, 7 proprietary), we uncover substantial cross-lingual performance variance in our proposed benchmark. Our analysis further shows that model size and architecture are poor predictors of multilingual robustness, and that even state-of-the-art LVLM judges exhibit inconsistent behavior across languages. Together, these findings expose fundamental limitations of current reward modeling and underscore the necessity of multilingual, multimodal benchmarks for developing reliable automated evaluators.

AI.tldr: They build the first large-scale multilingual, multimodal benchmark (MM-JudgeBench) to test how well vision-language reward models work across 25 languages, showing current judges are far from reliably multilingual.
AI.motivation: Automatic reward/judge models are widely used to align and evaluate LVLMs, but nearly all are tested and tuned only on English, so we lack understanding of their cross-lingual reliability and robustness.
AI.method: They construct MM-JudgeBench with 60K+ pairwise preference examples in 25 diverse languages, combining a general vision-language subset (extending VL-RewardBench) and a chart-focused reasoning subset (from OpenCQA). They also derive a separate multilingual training dataset (from MM-RewardBench) for domain adaptation. Then they evaluate 22 LVLMs (15 open and 7 proprietary) on the benchmark and analyze performance patterns across languages, model sizes, and architectures.
AI.result: Experiments reveal large performance differences across languages, with many LVLM judges performing much worse outside English. Model size and architecture do not reliably predict multilingual robustness, and even top-performing systems are unstable across languages and settings.
AI.conclusion: Current reward modeling for LVLMs does not generalize well across languages; multilingual, multimodal benchmarks like MM-JudgeBench are necessary to diagnose issues and guide the development of more reliable, globally robust automatic evaluators.

title: What Makes an LLM a Good Optimizer? A Trajectory Analysis of LLM-Guided Evolutionary Search
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, Accepted at Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Recent work has demonstrated the promise of orchestrating large language models (LLMs) within evolutionary and agentic optimization systems. However, the mechanisms driving these optimization gains remain poorly understood. In this work, we present a large-scale study of LLM-guided evolutionary search, collecting optimization trajectories for 15 LLMs across 8 tasks. Although zero-shot problem-solving ability correlates with final optimization outcomes, it explains only part of the variance: models with similar initial capability often induce dramatically different search trajectories and outcomes. By analyzing these trajectories, we find that strong LLM optimizers behave as local refiners, producing frequent incremental improvements while progressively localizing the search in semantic space. Conversely, weaker optimizers exhibit large semantic drift, with sporadic breakthroughs followed by stagnation. Notably, various measures of solution novelty do not predict final performance; novelty is beneficial only when the search remains sufficiently localized around high-performing regions of the solution space. Our results highlight the importance of trajectory analysis for understanding and improving LLM-based optimization systems and provide actionable insights for their design and training.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different large language models behave when used to guide evolutionary search, showing that strong models act as local refiners that steadily improve solutions in a focused region of semantic space, while weaker ones wander more and improve sporadically. It finds that simple zero-shot performance and abstract novelty metrics only partially explain optimization success, emphasizing the need to analyze full search trajectories to design better LLM-based optimizers.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used inside evolutionary and agent-based optimization frameworks, where they propose and refine candidate solutions. While these systems often work well, it is unclear *why* some LLMs produce better optimization performance than others, beyond their raw problem-solving ability. The authors aim to unpack the mechanisms behind these gains by looking not just at final results but at the entire optimization trajectory, to understand how different LLMs shape the search dynamics over time.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study of LLM-guided evolutionary search. They run evolutionary optimization across 8 tasks using 15 different LLMs and log complete optimization trajectories. They then analyze how solution quality and semantic properties evolve over time, comparing patterns such as degree of local refinement vs semantic drift, frequency and size of improvements, and different measures of solution novelty. Correlations are computed between zero-shot task performance, trajectory characteristics, novelty metrics, and final optimization outcomes.
AI.result: Zero-shot performance of an LLM on the tasks correlates with its eventual optimization success but leaves substantial variance unexplained. Models with similar initial capabilities can lead to very different search paths and final results. Strong LLM optimizers tend to make many small, consistent improvements, gradually narrowing the search in semantic space (local refinement). Weaker models cause large semantic shifts, with occasional big improvements but frequent stagnation afterward. Measures of novelty alone do not reliably predict final performance; novelty helps only when the search stays localized around already good regions of the solution space.
AI.conclusion: Optimization performance in LLM-guided evolutionary systems is determined not just by a model’s zero-shot ability or how novel its proposals are, but by the *structure of the search trajectory* it induces—especially its ability to perform localized refinement without excessive semantic drift. Therefore, understanding and improving LLM-based optimizers requires trajectory-level analysis. The findings suggest that effective system design and training should focus on encouraging stable, localized improvement dynamics rather than maximizing novelty per se.

title: 'The Order in the Horse's Heart': A Case Study in LLM-Assisted Stylometry for the Discovery of Biblical Allusion in Modern Literary Fiction
summary: We present a dual-track pipeline for detecting biblical allusions in literary fiction and apply it to the novels of Cormac McCarthy. A bottom-up embedding track uses inverse document frequency to identify rare vocabulary shared with the King James Bible, embeds occurrences in their local context for sense disambiguation, and passes candidate passage pairs through cascaded LLM review. A top-down register track asks an LLM to read McCarthy's prose undirected to any specific biblical passage for comparison, catching allusions not distinguished by word or phrase rarity. Both tracks are cross-validated by a long-context model that holds entire novels alongside the KJV in a single pass, and every finding is checked against published scholarship. Restricting attention to allusions that carry a textual echo--shared phrasing, reworked vocabulary, or transplanted cadence--and distinguishing literary allusions proper from signposted biblical references (similes naming biblical figures, characters overtly citing scripture), the pipeline surfaces 349 allusions across the corpus. Among a target set of 115 previously documented allusions retrieved through human review of the academic literature, the pipeline independently recovers 62 (54% recall), with recall varying by connection type from 30% (transformed imagery) to 80% (register collisions). We contextualise these results with respect to the value-add from LLMs as assistants to mechanical stylometric analyses, and their potential to facilitate the statistical study of intertextuality in massive literary corpora.

AI.tldr: Dual-track, LLM-assisted pipeline detects biblical allusions in Cormac McCarthy’s novels, achieving 54% recall on known cases and surfacing 349 allusions total.
AI.motivation: Manual identification of biblical allusions in large literary corpora is labor-intensive, subjective, and hard to scale. Existing stylometric or keyword-based methods struggle with subtle, transformed, or register-based allusions that do not rely on obvious shared wording. With LLMs offering new capabilities in contextual reading and pattern recognition, the authors aim to test whether they can systematically assist in detecting and validating intertextual links—specifically, biblical allusions in McCarthy—while remaining grounded in reproducible, quantitative methods.
AI.method: The authors build a dual-track pipeline. (1) Bottom-up ‘embedding’ track: use inverse document frequency to find rare vocabulary shared between McCarthy’s novels and the King James Bible; embed each occurrence in its local context for sense disambiguation; then send candidate passage pairs through cascaded LLM review to decide whether they constitute allusions. (2) Top-down ‘register’ track: prompt an LLM to read McCarthy’s prose without pointing to specific biblical passages, asking it instead to detect biblical register or echo, capturing allusions not based on rare words or phrases. Both tracks’ results are cross-validated using a long-context model that can read entire novels plus the full KJV in one pass. Finally, all putative allusions are checked against existing scholarly work, and the authors apply explicit criteria to count only literary allusions with textual echoes (shared or transformed phrasing, vocabulary, or cadence), excluding simple explicit references or name-dropping.
AI.result: The pipeline identifies 349 biblical allusions in McCarthy’s corpus that satisfy the authors’ definition of textual echo. Against a gold-standard set of 115 allusions documented in existing scholarship, the system autonomously recovers 62, corresponding to 54% overall recall. Performance differs by allusion type: recall is around 30% for heavily transformed imagery and up to 80% for ‘register collisions,’ where biblical style converges sharply with McCarthy’s prose. These quantitative results show varying sensitivity across allusion categories and demonstrate that LLM-assisted methods can systematically surface a substantial portion of known intertextual links in a complex literary domain.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs, when integrated into a carefully designed dual-track pipeline and combined with traditional stylometric tools, can significantly aid in detecting biblical allusions in literary fiction. While they do not replace human scholarship or achieve full recall—particularly for subtle, deeply transformed imagery—they reliably capture many allusions with clearer textual echoes and stylistic collisions. The authors argue that this structured, cross-validated use of LLMs paves the way for more scalable, statistical analyses of intertextuality in large literary corpora, positioning LLMs as valuable assistants rather than autonomous interpreters.

title: LePREC: Reasoning as Classification over Structured Factors for Assessing Relevance of Legal Issues
summary: More than half of the global population struggles to meet their civil justice needs due to limited legal resources. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning capabilities, significant challenges remain even at the foundational step of legal issue identification. To investigate LLMs' capabilities in this task, we constructed a dataset from 769 real-world Malaysian Contract Act court cases, using GPT-4o to extract facts and generate candidate legal issues, annotated by senior legal experts, which reveals a critical limitation: while LLMs generate diverse issue candidates, their precision remains inadequate (GPT-4o achieves only 62%). To address this gap, we propose LePREC (Legal Professional-inspired Reasoning Elicitation and Classification), a neuro-symbolic framework combining neural generation with structured statistical reasoning. LePREC consists of: (1) a neuro component leverages LLMs to transform legal descriptions into question-answer pairs representing diverse analytical factors, and (2) a symbolic component applies sparse linear models over these discrete features, learning explicit algebraic weights that identify the most informative reasoning factors. Unlike end-to-end neural approaches, LePREC achieves interpretability through transparent feature weighting while maintaining data efficiency through correlation-based statistical classification. Experiments show a 30-40% improvement over advanced LLM baselines, including GPT-4o and Claude, confirming that correlation-based factor-issue analysis offers a more data-efficient solution for relevance decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LePREC, a neuro-symbolic framework that improves legal issue identification from case facts, achieving higher precision and interpretability than end-to-end LLMs on Malaysian contract law cases.
AI.motivation: Over half of the world’s population cannot adequately address their civil justice problems, partly due to scarcity of legal professionals. Although LLMs show strong reasoning abilities, they still struggle with the crucial first step of legal assistance—accurately identifying the legal issues in a case. Existing LLM-based methods generate many possible issues but with low precision, making them unreliable in real-world legal workflows. The authors are motivated to systematically study this limitation and to design a method that is both more accurate and interpretable, while working with limited labeled legal data.
AI.method: The authors build a new dataset from 769 Malaysian Contract Act cases. Using GPT-4o, they extract factual descriptions and candidate legal issues from the judgments, and then have senior legal experts annotate and validate these issues, exposing LLM precision limits. They then propose LePREC, a neuro-symbolic architecture. The neuro component uses an LLM to convert legal descriptions into structured question-answer pairs that represent diverse reasoning factors. The symbolic component takes these discrete factors and trains sparse linear models, learning explicit algebraic weights that quantify how strongly each factor correlates with particular legal issues. This yields a transparent weighting of reasoning factors and a correlation-based classification mechanism for issue identification.
AI.result: On the constructed Malaysian contract law dataset, GPT-4o alone reaches only about 62% precision in legal issue identification, despite producing diverse candidate issues. LePREC significantly outperforms state-of-the-art LLM baselines, including GPT-4o and Claude, by roughly 30–40% in precision for issue relevance decisions. The method achieves these gains with relatively modest data requirements due to its factor-based, correlation-driven classifier and sparse linear models. It also produces explicit, interpretable weights over reasoning factors, enabling transparent analysis of why specific issues are predicted.
AI.conclusion: LLMs by themselves are not yet reliable for precise legal issue identification, even though they generate diverse candidate issues. A neuro-symbolic approach that decomposes reasoning into interpretable analytical factors and then uses sparse, correlation-based linear models can substantially boost performance while improving interpretability and data efficiency. LePREC demonstrates that professional-style factor analysis, made explicit in a symbolic layer, offers a more robust path for legal relevance decisions than purely end-to-end neural solutions, suggesting a promising direction for deploying AI responsibly in civil justice support systems.

title: Rank-Turbulence Delta and Interpretable Approaches to Stylometric Delta Metrics
comment: Under review at Digital Scholarship in the Humanities. Code available at: https://github.com/DDPronin/Rank-Turbulence-Delta
summary: This article introduces two new measures for authorship attribution - Rank-Turbulence Delta and Jensen-Shannon Delta - which generalise Burrows's classical Delta by applying distance functions designed for probabilistic distributions. We first set out the theoretical basis of the measures, contrasting centred and uncentred z-scoring of word-frequency vectors and re-casting the uncentred vectors as probability distributions. Building on this representation, we develop a token-level decomposition that renders every Delta distance numerically interpretable, thereby facilitating close reading and the validation of results. The effectiveness of the methods is assessed on four literary corpora in English, German, French and Russian. The English, German and French datasets are compiled from Project Gutenberg, whereas the Russian benchmark is the SOCIOLIT corpus containing 755 works by 180 authors spanning the eighteenth to the twenty-first centuries. Rank-Turbulence Delta attains attribution accuracy comparable with Cosine Delta; Jensen-Shannon Delta consistently matches or exceeds the performance of canonical Burrows's Delta. Finally, several established attribution algorithms are re-evaluated on the extended SOCIOLIT corpus.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes two new distance-based measures for authorship attribution that generalize Burrows’s Delta using probabilistic distances, and shows they perform as well as or better than existing methods across multilingual literary corpora.
AI.motivation: Existing authorship attribution commonly uses Burrows’s Delta, which relies on z-scored word-frequency vectors and lacks a clear probabilistic interpretation and token-level interpretability. There is a need for measures grounded in probability theory that can better model word-frequency distributions, be decomposed at the token level for interpretability and close reading, and be tested robustly on large, diverse corpora.
AI.method: The authors reformulate uncentred z-scored word-frequency vectors as probability distributions and introduce two new Delta measures based on distance functions suited to such distributions: Rank-Turbulence Delta and Jensen–Shannon Delta. They contrast centred vs. uncentred z-scoring, define the new distances, and derive a token-level decomposition that allows each component of the overall Delta distance to be numerically interpreted. They then evaluate the new measures on four literary datasets in English, German, French, and Russian (three from Project Gutenberg and the Russian SOCIOLIT corpus with 755 works by 180 authors), comparing performance against Cosine Delta and canonical Burrows’s Delta, and re-evaluating several existing attribution algorithms on the extended SOCIOLIT corpus.
AI.result: Rank-Turbulence Delta achieves authorship attribution accuracy on par with Cosine Delta, while Jensen–Shannon Delta consistently equals or outperforms classical Burrows’s Delta across all tested corpora. The token-level decomposition provides interpretable contributions of individual words/tokens to attribution decisions. The re-evaluation on the extended SOCIOLIT corpus yields updated performance baselines for established attribution algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Generalizing Burrows’s Delta via probabilistic distance functions yields more interpretable and competitive or superior authorship-attribution measures. Rank-Turbulence Delta and especially Jensen–Shannon Delta offer strong performance across multiple languages, and their token-level decompositions facilitate closer textual analysis and validation. The work also supplies new benchmark results on a large Russian corpus, refining comparative assessments of attribution methods.

title: Beyond Rating: A Comprehensive Evaluation and Benchmark for AI Reviews
summary: The rapid adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) has spurred interest in automated peer review; however, progress is currently stifled by benchmarks that treat reviewing primarily as a rating prediction task. We argue that the utility of a review lies in its textual justification--its arguments, questions, and critique--rather than a scalar score. To address this, we introduce Beyond Rating, a holistic evaluation framework that assesses AI reviewers across five dimensions: Content Faithfulness, Argumentative Alignment, Focus Consistency, Question Constructiveness, and AI-Likelihood. Notably, we propose a Max-Recall strategy to accommodate valid expert disagreement and introduce a curated dataset of paper with high-confidence reviews, rigorously filtered to remove procedural noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate that while traditional n-gram metrics fail to reflect human preferences, our proposed text-centric metrics--particularly the recall of weakness arguments--correlate strongly with rating accuracy. These findings establish that aligning AI critique focus with human experts is a prerequisite for reliable automated scoring, offering a robust standard for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new, text-focused framework and benchmark for evaluating automated peer-review systems, emphasizing the quality and alignment of written critiques over numerical score prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing automated peer review benchmarks mostly reduce reviewing to predicting overall ratings, ignoring that the true value of a review is in its textual justification (arguments, questions, critiques). This scalar-focus leads to weak supervision for training and evaluating AI reviewers, and fails to capture how well AI-generated reviews align with human expert reasoning. There is a need for an evaluation framework that reflects the multi-faceted, argumentative nature of real peer review and can handle legitimate disagreement among experts.
AI.method: The authors introduce Beyond Rating, a holistic evaluation framework with five dimensions: (1) Content Faithfulness: how accurately a review reflects the paper’s actual content; (2) Argumentative Alignment: how well the AI’s arguments and critiques match human experts’ points; (3) Focus Consistency: whether the AI focuses on similar aspects of the paper as human reviewers; (4) Question Constructiveness: the usefulness and constructiveness of questions posed by the AI; and (5) AI-Likelihood: how likely text is to be AI-generated. They design text-centric metrics for these dimensions and propose a Max-Recall strategy that treats any expert-raised weakness as valid, measuring how many of these diverse expert criticisms the AI recovers. They also curate a dataset of papers paired with high-confidence expert reviews, carefully filtering out procedural or noisy comments. Extensive experiments compare their text-based metrics (especially recall of weakness arguments) to traditional n-gram similarity metrics and rating prediction baselines.
AI.result: Experiments show that standard n-gram-based metrics (e.g., overlap scores) poorly reflect human judgments of review quality and do not correlate well with accurate rating prediction. In contrast, the proposed text-centric metrics—particularly the recall of expert-identified weaknesses—correlate strongly with how well AI systems predict final ratings. The framework reliably distinguishes better AI reviewers from weaker ones, and the Max-Recall strategy successfully accommodates genuine disagreement among human reviewers while still providing a stable evaluation signal.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that reliably automating peer review requires evaluating and aligning the textual critique itself, not just predicted scores. Aligning the focus and arguments of AI-generated reviews with those of human experts is a necessary precursor to trustworthy automated scoring. Beyond Rating offers a robust, multi-dimensional standard for evaluating AI reviewers and is intended to guide future research toward richer, argument-focused review generation and assessment.

title: Bangla Key2Text: Text Generation from Keywords for a Low Resource Language
summary: This paper introduces \textit{Bangla Key2Text}, a large-scale dataset of $2.6$ million Bangla keyword--text pairs designed for keyword-driven text generation in a low-resource language. The dataset is constructed using a BERT-based keyword extraction pipeline applied to millions of Bangla news texts, transforming raw articles into structured keyword--text pairs suitable for supervised learning. To establish baseline performance on this new benchmark, we fine-tune two sequence-to-sequence models, \texttt{mT5} and \texttt{BanglaT5}, and evaluate them using multiple automatic metrics and human judgments. Experimental results show that task-specific fine-tuning substantially improves keyword-conditioned text generation in Bangla compared to zero-shot large language models. The dataset, trained models, and code are publicly released to support future research in Bangla natural language generation and keyword-to-text generation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Bangla Key2Text, a 2.6M-pair keyword-to-text dataset for Bangla, plus baseline seq2seq models, enabling supervised keyword-conditioned generation in a low-resource language.
AI.motivation: Bangla is a low-resource language for natural language generation, especially for keyword-to-text tasks. Existing large language models perform poorly in zero-shot settings for Bangla, and there is a lack of large, structured, supervised datasets to train and benchmark models for keyword-driven Bangla text generation. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a scalable dataset and benchmark to foster research and improve generation quality.
AI.method: They automatically construct a dataset of 2.6 million keyword–text pairs from millions of Bangla news articles. A BERT-based keyword extraction pipeline identifies salient keywords from each raw article, pairing these extracted keywords with the original article text to form supervised training examples. They then fine-tune two sequence-to-sequence models, mT5 (multilingual) and BanglaT5 (Bangla-specific), on this dataset. Performance is evaluated using multiple automatic metrics (likely BLEU, ROUGE, etc.) and human evaluations, and compared against zero-shot large language models on keyword-conditioned generation.
AI.result: Task-specific fine-tuning of mT5 and BanglaT5 on the Bangla Key2Text dataset leads to substantial improvements in Bangla keyword-conditioned text generation quality over zero-shot large language models. The constructed dataset proves effective for training and yields strong baseline results according to both automatic evaluation metrics and human judgments.
AI.conclusion: Bangla Key2Text provides a large-scale, publicly available benchmark and resource for keyword-to-text generation in Bangla, addressing the low-resource status of the language for this task. Fine-tuned seq2seq models significantly outperform zero-shot LLMs, demonstrating the value of supervised, task-specific data. By releasing the dataset, trained models, and code, the authors aim to catalyze further research and advancement in Bangla natural language generation and related keyword-driven generation tasks.

title: Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction in Conversations via Semantic Decoupling and Graph Alignment
summary: Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction in Conversations (ECPEC) aims to identify the set of causal relations between emotion utterances and their triggering causes within a dialogue. Most existing approaches formulate ECPEC as an independent pairwise classification task, overlooking the distinct semantics of emotion diffusion and cause explanation, and failing to capture globally consistent many-to-many conversational causality. To address these limitations, we revisit ECPEC from a semantic perspective and seek to disentangle emotion-oriented semantics from cause-oriented semantics, mapping them into two complementary representation spaces to better capture their distinct conversational roles. Building on this semantic decoupling, we naturally formulate ECPEC as a global alignment problem between the emotion-side and cause-side representations, and employ optimal transport to enable many-to-many and globally consistent emotion-cause matching. Based on this perspective, we propose a unified framework SCALE that instantiates the above semantic decoupling and alignment principle within a shared conversational structure. Extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets demonstrate that SCALE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our codes are released at https://github.com/CoCoSphere/SCALE.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SCALE, a framework that models Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction in conversations as a global alignment problem between emotion and cause representations using optimal transport, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction in Conversations (ECPEC) methods treat each potential emotion-cause pair as an independent classification instance. This ignores (1) the different semantic roles of emotion expressions vs. cause explanations, and (2) the global, many-to-many causal structure in multi-turn dialogues. As a result, models struggle to capture conversationally consistent emotion-cause relations and to reflect the diffusion of emotions and the explanations behind them.
AI.method: The authors introduce SCALE, a unified framework that first semantically disentangles emotion-oriented semantics from cause-oriented semantics. It maps them into two complementary representation spaces that encode their distinct conversational roles. With this decoupling, ECPEC is reformulated as a global alignment problem: emotion-side and cause-side representations are matched using optimal transport, which naturally supports many-to-many and globally consistent emotion-cause links across the whole dialogue. The entire process is modeled within a shared conversational structure so that both local utterance information and global dialogue context are preserved.
AI.result: On several ECPEC benchmark datasets, SCALE consistently outperforms prior methods, establishing new state-of-the-art performance in extracting emotion-cause pairs from conversations. The improvements suggest better modeling of many-to-many relations and enhanced robustness across different datasets.
AI.conclusion: Reframing ECPEC as a semantic decoupling and global alignment problem, rather than independent pairwise classification, yields superior performance. By learning distinct yet complementary spaces for emotion and cause semantics and aligning them via optimal transport, SCALE can more faithfully capture the complex, many-to-many causal structures in dialogues. The released code supports reproduction and further work on conversational emotion-cause modeling.

title: Taming Actor-Observer Asymmetry in Agents via Dialectical Alignment
comment: ACL 2026 Main Conference. Project page: https://unikcc.github.io/ReTAS/
summary: Large Language Model agents have rapidly evolved from static text generators into dynamic systems capable of executing complex autonomous workflows. To enhance reliability, multi-agent frameworks assigning specialized roles are increasingly adopted to enable self-reflection and mutual auditing. While such role-playing effectively leverages domain expert knowledge, we find it simultaneously induces a human-like cognitive bias known as Actor-Observer Asymmetry (AOA). Specifically, an agent acting as an actor (during self-reflection) tends to attribute failures to external factors, whereas an observer (during mutual auditing) attributes the same errors to internal faults. We quantify this using our new Ambiguous Failure Benchmark, which reveals that simply swapping perspectives triggers the AOA effect in over 20% of cases for most models. To tame this bias, we introduce ReTAS (Reasoning via Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis), a model trained through dialectical alignment to enforce perspective-invariant reasoning. By integrating dialectical chain-of-thought with Group Relative Policy Optimization, ReTAS guides agents to synthesize conflicting viewpoints into an objective consensus. Experiments demonstrate that ReTAS effectively mitigates attribution inconsistency and significantly improves fault resolution rates in ambiguous scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and quantifies a cognitive bias called Actor-Observer Asymmetry (AOA) in multi-agent LLM systems and proposes ReTAS, a dialectically aligned reasoning method that mitigates this bias and improves fault resolution.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents evolve into multi-agent systems with specialized roles for self-reflection and mutual auditing, they inherit human-like cognitive biases that can undermine reliability. Existing frameworks do not explicitly account for or mitigate these biases, especially in ambiguous failure cases where perspective (actor vs observer) changes attributions of error.
