AI.method: 1) Define and study Actor-Observer Asymmetry (AOA) in multi-agent LLM settings. 2) Construct an Ambiguous Failure Benchmark where the same error can be plausibly attributed to internal or external causes, and use it to measure how role perspective (actor vs. observer) changes attributions. 3) Show that merely swapping perspectives induces AOA in over 20% of cases in common LLMs. 4) Propose ReTAS, a dialectical alignment method using a Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis reasoning scheme combined with Group Relative Policy Optimization to enforce perspective-invariant, consensus-oriented reasoning. 5) Evaluate ReTAS on the benchmark, focusing on attribution consistency and fault resolution rates.
AI.result: AOA is prevalent in multi-agent LLM systems: swapping between actor and observer roles leads to systematically different attributions in more than 20% of evaluated cases on the Ambiguous Failure Benchmark. The proposed ReTAS framework significantly reduces this attribution inconsistency and improves the rate at which agents correctly identify and resolve faults in ambiguous scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Human-like cognitive biases such as Actor-Observer Asymmetry naturally emerge in role-based multi-agent LLM systems and can harm reliability in fault diagnosis. By training models with dialectical alignment via ReTAS—integrating thesis-antithesis-synthesis reasoning and group policy optimization—agents achieve more perspective-invariant, objective reasoning, leading to better fault attribution and resolution in ambiguous tasks.

title: Detecting Hallucinations in SpeechLLMs at Inference Time Using Attention Maps
summary: Hallucinations in Speech Large Language Models (SpeechLLMs) pose significant risks, yet existing detection methods typically rely on gold-standard outputs that are costly or impractical to obtain. Moreover, hallucination detection methods developed for text-based LLMs do not directly capture audio-specific signals. We investigate four attention-derived metrics: AUDIORATIO, AUDIOCONSISTENCY, AUDIOENTROPY, and TEXTENTROPY, designed to capture pathological attention patterns associated with hallucination, and train lightweight logistic regression classifiers on these features for efficient inference-time detection. Across automatic speech recognition and speech-to-text translation tasks, evaluations on Qwen-2-Audio and Voxtral-3B show that our approach outperforms uncertainty-based and prior attention-based baselines on in-domain data, achieving improvements of up to +0.23 PR-AUC, and generalises to out-of-domain ASR settings. We further find that strong performance can be achieved with approximately 100 attention heads, improving out-of-domain generalisation compared to using all heads. While effectiveness is model-dependent and task-specific training is required, our results demonstrate that attention patterns provide a valuable tool for hallucination detection in SpeechLLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes attention-based metrics to detect hallucinations in Speech Large Language Models without needing reference transcripts, showing better performance and generalization than prior uncertainty and attention-based methods.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in SpeechLLMs are dangerous, and existing detectors either need expensive gold labels or are designed for text-only LLMs and ignore audio-specific cues. The authors want a cheap, reference-free, audio-aware way to flag hallucinated outputs at inference time.
AI.method: They define four metrics derived from model attention patterns—AUDIORATIO, AUDIOCONSISTENCY, AUDIOENTROPY, and TEXTENTROPY—to capture abnormal focus over audio and text tokens. These metrics are used as features in lightweight logistic regression classifiers, trained separately for different tasks, to predict whether a given output is hallucinated. They experiment on automatic speech recognition and speech-to-text translation, using models Qwen-2-Audio and Voxtral-3B, and vary how many attention heads are used.
AI.result: Their attention-metric classifiers outperform uncertainty-based and prior attention-based baselines on in-domain ASR and speech translation, improving PR-AUC by up to 0.23. The approach also transfers to out-of-domain ASR data, and using around 100 attention heads yields strong performance and better out-of-domain generalization than using all heads.
AI.conclusion: Attention patterns in SpeechLLMs encode useful signals for hallucination detection that can be exploited by simple classifiers without references. Although performance depends on the underlying model and requires task-specific training, a subset of attention heads suffices to achieve accurate and more robust hallucination detection across domains.

title: A Self-Evolving Framework for Efficient Terminal Agents via Observational Context Compression
summary: As model capabilities advance, research has increasingly shifted toward long-horizon, multi-turn terminal-centric agentic tasks, where raw environment feedback is often preserved in the interaction history to support future decisions. However, repeatedly retaining such feedback introduces substantial redundancy and causes cumulative token cost to grow quadratically with the number of steps, hindering long-horizon reasoning. Although observation compression can mitigate this issue, the heterogeneity of terminal environments makes heuristic-based or fixed-prompt methods difficult to generalize. We propose TACO, a plug-and-play, self-evolving Terminal Agent Compression framework that automatically discovers and refines compression rules from interaction trajectories for existing terminal agents. Experiments on TerminalBench (TB 1.0 and TB 2.0) and four additional terminal-related benchmarks (i.e., SWE-Bench Lite, CompileBench, DevEval, and CRUST-Bench) show that TACO consistently improves performance across mainstream agent frameworks and strong backbone models. With MiniMax-2.5, it improves performance on most benchmarks while reducing token overhead by around 10%. On TerminalBench, it brings consistent gains of 1%-4% across strong agentic models, and further improves accuracy by around 2%-3% under the same token budget. These results demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of self-evolving, task-aware compression for terminal agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TACO, a framework that automatically learns how to compress terminal interaction histories for long-horizon agents, cutting token cost while slightly improving performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon terminal-based agents repeatedly store raw environment feedback in their context, leading to quadratic growth in token usage over many steps. This redundancy makes long-horizon reasoning expensive and inefficient. Existing observation-compression approaches rely on heuristics or fixed prompts that are hard to generalize across heterogeneous terminal environments. The authors want a general, automatic way to compress histories that preserves important information while reducing token cost.
AI.method: They propose TACO, a plug-and-play, self-evolving compression framework for terminal agents. TACO automatically discovers and refines task-aware compression rules from agent interaction trajectories instead of relying on hand-crafted heuristics. It is designed to integrate with existing terminal agent frameworks and backbone language models, learning how to summarize or filter terminal feedback so that only useful information is kept for future steps. The compression rules evolve over time as more trajectories are observed, making the system adaptive to different environments and tasks.
AI.result: On TerminalBench (TB 1.0 and TB 2.0) and four additional terminal-related benchmarks (SWE-Bench Lite, CompileBench, DevEval, and CRUST-Bench), TACO consistently improves task performance while reducing token usage. With the MiniMax-2.5 model, it reduces token overhead by about 10% and still improves scores on most benchmarks. On TerminalBench specifically, TACO yields performance gains of 1%–4% across strong agentic models, and achieves about 2%–3% higher accuracy under the same token budget compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Self-evolving, task-aware compression of terminal interaction histories is both effective and generalizable. TACO can be plugged into existing terminal agents to automatically learn compression strategies that cut token costs and slightly boost or at least maintain performance across diverse benchmarks. This suggests that learned, adaptive compression is a practical solution for scaling long-horizon, multi-turn terminal-centric agent tasks.

title: Impact of large language models on peer review opinions from a fine-grained perspective: Evidence from top conference proceedings in AI
summary: With the rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), the academic community has faced unprecedented disruptions, particularly in the realm of academic communication. The primary function of peer review is improving the quality of academic manuscripts, such as clarity, originality and other evaluation aspects. Although prior studies suggest that LLMs are beginning to influence peer review, it remains unclear whether they are altering its core evaluative functions. Moreover, the extent to which LLMs affect the linguistic form, evaluative focus, and recommendation-related signals of peer-review reports has yet to be systematically examined. In this study, we examine the changes in peer review reports for academic articles following the emergence of LLMs, emphasizing variations at fine-grained level. Specifically, we investigate linguistic features such as the length and complexity of words and sentences in review comments, while also automatically annotating the evaluation aspects of individual review sentences. We also use a maximum likelihood estimation method, previously established, to identify review reports that potentially have modified or generated by LLMs. Finally, we assess the impact of evaluation aspects mentioned in LLM-assisted review reports on the informativeness of recommendation for paper decision-making. The results indicate that following the emergence of LLMs, peer review texts have become longer and more fluent, with increased emphasis on summaries and surface-level clarity, as well as more standardized linguistic patterns, particularly reviewers with lower confidence score. At the same time, attention to deeper evaluative dimensions, such as originality, replicability, and nuanced critical reasoning, has declined.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how the rise of LLMs has changed the content and style of peer-review reports, finding longer, more fluent but shallower reviews with less focus on deep evaluation dimensions.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in academic workflows, including peer review, but it is unclear whether and how they change the core evaluative function of reviews (clarity, originality, etc.). The authors want to understand whether LLM assistance is reshaping what reviewers focus on and how they write, which has implications for research quality and editorial decision-making.
AI.method: The authors analyze peer-review reports for academic articles before and after the emergence of LLMs. They conduct fine-grained linguistic analysis (e.g., length and complexity of words and sentences), automatically annotate each review sentence with evaluation aspects (e.g., clarity, originality), and apply an existing maximum likelihood estimation method to detect reviews likely modified or generated by LLMs. They then model how the presence of particular evaluation aspects in LLM-assisted reviews affects the informativeness of the recommendation for accept/reject decisions.
AI.result: After LLMs became widely available, peer review texts became longer and more fluent, with more standardized language. Reviewers—especially those with lower confidence scores—place more emphasis on summaries and surface-level clarity. However, there is a reduction in attention to deeper evaluative aspects such as originality, replicability, and nuanced critical reasoning. These changes alter the balance between superficial polish and substantive critique in peer reviews and influence the strength and informativeness of reviewers’ recommendations.
AI.conclusion: LLM assistance in peer review is associated with more polished and verbose reports but a weakening of core evaluative dimensions essential for scientific quality, such as originality and replicability. While LLMs may improve linguistic quality, they risk diluting the depth of critical assessment. Editorial policies and reviewer guidelines may need to adapt to ensure that LLM use does not compromise the substantive evaluative function of peer review.

title: A Bolu: A Structured Dataset for the Computational Analysis of Sardinian Improvisational Poetry
comment: Accepted at the DIALRES Workshop, LREC-COLING 2026
summary: The growing interest of Natural Language Processing (NLP) in minority languages has not yet bridged the gap in the preservation of oral linguistic heritage. In particular, extemporaneous poetry - a performative genre based on real-time improvisation, metrical-rhetorical competence - remains a largely unexplored area of computational linguistics. This methodological gap necessitates the creation of specific resources to document and analyse the structures of improvised poetry. This is the context in which A Bolu was created, the first structured corpus of extemporaneous poetry dedicated to cantada logudorese, a variant of the Sardinian language. The dataset comprises 2,835 stanzas for a total of 141,321 tokens. The study presents the architecture of the corpus and applies a multidimensional analysis combining descriptive statistical indices and computational linguistics techniques to map the characteristics of the poetic text. The results indicate that the production of Sardinian extemporaneous poets is characterised by recurring patterns that support Parry and Lord's theory of formulaicity. This evidence not only provides a new key to understanding oral creativity, but also offers a significant contribution to the development of NLP tools that are more inclusive and sensitive to the specificities of less widely spoken languages.

AI.tldr: They build and analyze the first structured corpus of improvised Sardinian poetry to reveal recurring formulaic patterns and support better NLP for minority oral traditions.
AI.motivation: NLP has increasingly focused on minority languages, but mainly on written text. Oral, improvised poetic traditions—especially extemporaneous poetry—remain under-documented and under-analyzed, which hinders both linguistic preservation and the development of NLP models that can handle these genres. There is a need for specific, well-structured resources to systematically study the metrical, rhetorical, and improvisational properties of such oral poetry.
AI.method: The authors construct “A Bolu,” a structured corpus of extemporaneous poetry in cantada logudorese, a Sardinian variety. The corpus contains 2,835 stanzas and 141,321 tokens. They then perform a multidimensional analysis that combines descriptive statistics (e.g., token counts, frequency patterns) with computational linguistics techniques to identify and map structural, lexical, and formulaic characteristics of the poetic text.
AI.result: The analysis reveals recurrent linguistic and structural patterns in the improvised poems. These recurring patterns align with Parry and Lord’s theory of formulaicity, which posits that oral poets rely on reusable formulas and patterns to compose in real time. The corpus thus demonstrates that Sardinian extemporaneous poets systematically exploit formulaic structures in their performances.
AI.conclusion: A Bolu fills a key resource gap by providing the first structured corpus of Sardinian extemporaneous poetry and shows that this oral tradition is highly formulaic. This supports classic theories of oral composition and offers empirical evidence that can inform more inclusive NLP tools tailored to the characteristics of minority and oral languages, improving both documentation and computational modeling of such traditions.

title: RoLegalGEC: Legal Domain Grammatical Error Detection and Correction Dataset for Romanian
summary: The importance of clear and correct text in legal documents cannot be understated, and, consequently, a grammatical error correction tool meant to assist a professional in the law must have the ability to understand the possible errors in the context of a legal environment, correcting them accordingly, and implicitly needs to be trained in the same environment, using realistic legal data. However, the manually annotated data required by such a process is in short supply for languages such as Romanian, much less for a niche domain. The most common approach is the synthetic generation of parallel data; however, it requires a structured understanding of the Romanian grammar. In this paper, we introduce, to our knowledge, the first Romanian-language parallel dataset for the detection and correction of grammatical errors in the legal domain, RoLegalGEC, which aggregates 350,000 examples of errors in legal passages, along with error annotations. Moreover, we evaluate several neural network models that transform the dataset into a valuable tool for both detecting and correcting grammatical errors, including knowledge-distillation Transformers, sequence tagging architectures for detection, and a variety of pre-trained text-to-text Transformer models for correction. We consider that the set of models, together with the novel RoLegalGEC dataset, will enrich the resource base for further research on Romanian.

AI.tldr: They create the first Romanian legal-domain dataset for grammatical error detection and correction and benchmark multiple neural models on it.
AI.motivation: Legal documents require extremely precise and correct language, but existing grammatical error correction tools are not trained on Romanian legal text, and manually annotated legal data in Romanian is scarce. To build effective tools for this niche domain, there is a need for realistic, domain-specific parallel data capturing typical grammatical errors in Romanian legal language.
AI.method: They construct RoLegalGEC, a parallel Romanian dataset focused on the legal domain, by aggregating and annotating 350,000 examples of grammatical errors in legal passages. They then train and evaluate several neural architectures on this dataset: knowledge-distillation Transformer models, sequence tagging models for error detection, and various pre-trained text-to-text Transformer models for error correction.
AI.result: RoLegalGEC comprises 350,000 parallel examples of legal Romanian text with grammatical errors and their corrections, including explicit error annotations. Neural models trained on this dataset can both detect and correct grammatical errors, demonstrating the dataset’s usefulness and turning the models into practical tools for legal-language GEC.
AI.conclusion: RoLegalGEC is, to the authors’ knowledge, the first Romanian legal-domain grammatical error correction and detection dataset. Combined with the evaluated neural models, it provides a valuable new resource for Romanian NLP, particularly for developing accurate grammar tools for legal professionals and for enabling further research in this under-resourced language and niche domain.

title: Cross-Model Consistency of AI-Generated Exercise Prescriptions: A Repeated Generation Study Across Three Large Language Models
comment: 24 Pages, 2 Figures, 6 Tables and 2 Supplementary Materials
summary: This study compared repeated generation consistency of exercise prescription outputs across three large language models (LLMs), specifically GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Gemini 2.5 Flash, under temperature=0 conditions. Each model generated prescriptions for six clinical scenarios 20 times, yielding 360 total outputs analyzed across four dimensions: semantic similarity, output reproducibility, FITT classification, and safety expression. Mean semantic similarity was highest for GPT-4.1 (0.955), followed by Gemini 2.5 Flash (0.950) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (0.903), with significant inter-model differences confirmed (H = 458.41, p < .001). Critically, these scores reflected fundamentally different generative behaviors: GPT-4.1 produced entirely unique outputs (100%) with stable semantic content, while Gemini 2.5 Flash showed pronounced output repetition (27.5% unique outputs), indicating that its high similarity score derived from text duplication rather than consistent reasoning. Identical decoding settings thus yielded fundamentally different consistency profiles, a distinction that single-output evaluations cannot capture. Safety expression reached ceiling levels across all models, confirming its limited utility as a differentiating metric. These results indicate that model selection constitutes a clinical rather than merely technical decision, and that output behavior under repeated generation conditions should be treated as a core criterion for reliable deployment of LLM-based exercise prescription systems.

AI.tldr: The paper compares how consistently three LLMs generate exercise prescriptions across repeated runs, showing that models can have similar semantic similarity scores for very different reasons (unique-but-consistent vs repetitive outputs), so repeated-generation behavior must be evaluated for clinical deployment.
AI.motivation: To determine whether different LLMs behave consistently and reliably when repeatedly generating exercise prescriptions for clinical scenarios, since clinical use requires stable reasoning rather than just single high-quality outputs.
AI.method: Three LLMs (GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash) were tested with temperature=0 on six clinical exercise-prescription scenarios. Each model generated 20 prescriptions per scenario (360 outputs total). Outputs were analyzed for semantic similarity, textual reproducibility/uniqueness, FITT (frequency, intensity, time, type) classification structure, and presence of safety-related language. Statistical tests (e.g., Kruskal–Wallis H-test) assessed inter-model differences.
AI.result: GPT-4.1 showed the highest mean semantic similarity (0.955) and produced 100% unique outputs that still conveyed consistent underlying content. Gemini 2.5 Flash had slightly lower mean similarity (0.950) but only 27.5% unique outputs, indicating heavy repetition; its high similarity stemmed from output duplication rather than stable reasoning. Claude Sonnet 4.6 showed the lowest similarity (0.903). Safety language was at ceiling for all models, offering little discriminative value. Identical decoding settings nonetheless produced markedly different consistency and reproducibility profiles across models.
AI.conclusion: LLMs with similar average semantic similarity scores can have very different generative behaviors under repeated prompts. For clinical exercise-prescription systems, it is not enough to evaluate single outputs or aggregate similarity alone; one must examine repeated-generation behavior, uniqueness vs repetition, and content stability. Consequently, model selection is a substantive clinical decision factor, and repeated-output profiling should be a core part of validation for LLM tools in healthcare contexts.

title: The "Small World of Words" German Free-Association Norms
summary: Free-association norms provide essential empirical data for investigating linguistic, semantic, and cultural phenomena in the cognitive sciences. Although large-scale norms exist for languages such as English, Dutch, Spanish, and Mandarin Chinese, no comparable resource has been available for German. To address this gap, we present free-association norms for 5,877 German cue words as part of the German version of the multilingual Small World of Words (SWOW) project. We describe the data collection procedures, participant characteristics, and our comprehensive preprocessing pipeline before introducing the resulting SWOW-DE data set. Using data from three established psycholinguistic paradigms, we show that SWOW-DE norms robustly predict performance in lexical decision tasks, relatedness judgments, and psycholinguistic word ratings. Furthermore, we demonstrate that SWOW-DE responses compare favorably with existing German resources and provide a preliminary cross-linguistic comparison revealing both shared and language-specific association patterns, highlighting promising directions for future research. Overall, SWOW-DE represents the largest collection of German free associations to date and offers a unique resource for linguistic, psychological, and cross-cultural research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SWOW-DE, the largest German free-association norms dataset, and demonstrates its usefulness in predicting psycholinguistic behavior and enabling cross-linguistic comparisons.
AI.motivation: There is no large-scale free-association norms resource for German, unlike for English, Dutch, Spanish, and Mandarin, limiting research on German linguistic, semantic, and cultural phenomena and hampering cross-linguistic comparisons.
AI.method: The authors collected free associations for 5,877 German cue words within the Small World of Words framework, documented participant characteristics and collection procedures, applied a comprehensive preprocessing pipeline, and evaluated the dataset by testing how well the norms predict performance in lexical decision, relatedness judgment, and word-rating paradigms, as well as by comparing SWOW-DE to existing German resources and other languages’ association norms.
AI.result: The resulting SWOW-DE dataset contains large-scale free-association norms for 5,877 German cues. These norms robustly predict behavior in lexical decision tasks, semantic relatedness judgments, and psycholinguistic word ratings, and compare favorably with existing German resources. Initial cross-linguistic analyses show both shared and language-specific association patterns.
AI.conclusion: SWOW-DE is the largest German free-association resource available, empirically valid across multiple psycholinguistic paradigms, and valuable for linguistic, psychological, and cross-cultural research, with particular promise for cross-linguistic comparisons of semantic networks.

title: Micro Language Models Enable Instant Responses
summary: Edge devices such as smartwatches and smart glasses cannot continuously run even the smallest 100M-1B parameter language models due to power and compute constraints, yet cloud inference introduces multi-second latencies that break the illusion of a responsive assistant. We introduce micro language models ($μ$LMs): ultra-compact models (8M-30M parameters) that instantly generate the first 4-8 words of a contextually grounded response on-device, while a cloud model completes it; thus, masking the cloud latency. We show that useful language generation survives at this extreme scale with our models matching several 70M-256M-class existing models. We design a collaborative generation framework that reframes the cloud model as a continuator rather than a respondent, achieving seamless mid-sentence handoffs and structured graceful recovery via three error correction methods when the local opener goes wrong. Empirical results show that $μ$LMs can initiate responses that larger models complete seamlessly, demonstrating that orders-of-magnitude asymmetric collaboration is achievable and unlocking responsive AI for extremely resource-constrained devices. The model checkpoint and demo are available at https://github.com/Sensente/micro_language_model_swen_project.

AI.tldr: Introduces ultra-small on-device language models (8M–30M parameters) that generate only the first few words of a response locally, then hand off to a cloud model, hiding cloud latency.
AI.motivation: Edge devices like smartwatches and smart glasses cannot run even small modern LLMs continuously due to power and compute limits, while full cloud inference causes noticeable multi-second delays that ruin the feeling of an instant, responsive assistant. There is a need for a hybrid approach that preserves responsiveness on very constrained hardware without sacrificing the quality of larger cloud models.
AI.method: Design “micro language models” (μLMs), extremely compact LLMs (8M–30M parameters) trained to produce short, contextually appropriate response openings. Propose a collaborative generation framework where the μLM runs fully on-device to output the first 4–8 tokens almost instantly, and a large cloud model then continues generation mid-sentence. Treat the cloud model as a continuator instead of a full responder and introduce three error-correction strategies to recover gracefully when the local model’s opening is suboptimal or incorrect, enabling smooth handoff and correction.
AI.result: Experiments show that μLMs at only 8M–30M parameters can still produce useful, contextually grounded openings and match or exceed the performance of some much larger 70M–256M-class models for this specific opening task. The collaborative scheme supports seamless mid-sentence handoff, with larger models able to continue the μLM’s partial outputs without noticeable artifacts, and error-correction mechanisms effectively mitigate cases where the μLM’s output is poor. Orders-of-magnitude asymmetric collaboration between edge and cloud models is empirically validated.
AI.conclusion: Extreme downsizing of on-device models is viable when they are tasked only with generating short response prefixes and are paired with powerful cloud models as continuators. This asymmetric collaborative framework hides cloud latency, preserves the illusion of instant responses, and enables responsive AI assistants even on highly resource-constrained edge devices. The released checkpoints and demo provide a practical path for deploying such systems in real-world applications.

title: The signal is the ceiling: Measurement limits of LLM-predicted experience ratings from open-ended survey text
summary: An earlier paper (Hong, Potteiger, and Zapata 2026) established that an unoptimized GPT 4.1 prompt predicts fan-reported experience ratings within one point 67% of the time from open-ended survey text. This paper tests the relative impact of prompt design and model selection on that performance. We compared four configurations on approximately 10,000 post-game surveys from five MLB teams: the original baseline prompt and a moderately customized version, crossed with three GPT models (4.1, 4.1-mini, 5.2). Prompt customization added roughly two percentage points of within +/-1 agreement on GPT 4.1 (from 67% to 69%). Both model swaps from that best configuration degraded performance: GPT 5.2 returned to the baseline, and GPT 4.1-mini fell six percentage points below it. Both levers combined were dwarfed by the input itself: across capable configurations, accuracy varied more than an order of magnitude more by the linguistic character of the text than by the choice of prompt or model. The ceiling has two parts. One is a bias in how the model reads text, which prompt design can correct. The other is a difference between what fans write about and what they actually decide, which no engineering can close because the missing information is not in the text. Prompt customization moved the first part; model selection moved neither reliably. The result is not that "prompt engineering helps a little" but that prompt engineering helps in a specific and predictable way, on the part of the ceiling it can reach.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how much prompt customization and model choice affect the ability of GPT models to predict fan experience ratings from open-ended MLB survey text, finding small gains from prompt design, mixed or negative effects from model swapping, and much larger limits imposed by the information content of the text itself.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed an unoptimized GPT‑4.1 prompt could predict fan-reported experience ratings within one point about two-thirds of the time. However, it was unclear how much of this performance depended on the specific prompt versus the choice of model, and what the upper limits of this approach were given real survey data. Practitioners need to know where to invest effort—prompt engineering, upgrading models, or improving data collection—to enhance prediction quality.
AI.method: The authors evaluate four GPT configurations on ~10,000 post‑game survey responses from five MLB teams. They cross two prompt designs (the original baseline prompt and a moderately customized version) with three models (GPT‑4.1, GPT‑4.1‑mini, GPT‑5.2). Performance is measured as the percentage of model-predicted ratings that fall within ±1 point of the fan’s self‑reported experience rating. They also analyze how performance varies as a function of the linguistic characteristics of the input text to quantify how much variance is driven by prompts, models, and the data itself.
AI.result: Prompt customization on GPT‑4.1 improves within‑±1 agreement from 67% to 69%, a modest ~2‑point gain. Changing models from this best configuration does not help: GPT‑5.2 drops back to the 67% baseline, while GPT‑4.1‑mini performs about six points worse than the baseline. Across all reasonably capable setups, variation in accuracy driven by differences in the survey text is more than an order of magnitude larger than the variation due to either prompt design or model choice. This reveals a strong data‑driven performance ceiling.
AI.conclusion: Prompt engineering yields small but consistent improvements by correcting systematic biases in how the model interprets text, moving one component of the performance ceiling. Model upgrades, at least among the tested GPT variants, do not reliably move that ceiling. The largest constraint comes from what fans choose to write: parts of their actual experience and decisions are simply not encoded in text, creating an irreducible gap between predictions and true ratings. Thus, prompt engineering is helpful in a specific, predictable way on the portion of the ceiling within reach, whereas neither prompt design nor model selection can overcome missing information in the input data.

title: Chat2Workflow: A Benchmark for Generating Executable Visual Workflows with Natural Language
summary: At present, executable visual workflows have emerged as a mainstream paradigm in real-world industrial deployments, offering strong reliability and controllability. However, in current practice, such workflows are almost entirely constructed through manual engineering: developers must carefully design workflows, write prompts for each step, and repeatedly revise the logic as requirements evolve-making development costly, time-consuming, and error-prone. To study whether large language models can automate this multi-round interaction process, we introduce Chat2Workflow, a benchmark for generating executable visual workflows directly from natural language, and propose a robust agentic framework to mitigate recurrent execution errors. Chat2Workflow is built from a large collection of real-world business workflows, with each instance designed so that the generated workflow can be transformed and directly deployed to practical workflow platforms such as Dify and Coze. Experimental results show that while state-of-the-art language models can often capture high-level intent, they struggle to generate correct, stable, and executable workflows, especially under complex or changing requirements. Although our agentic framework yields up to 5.34% resolve rate gains, the remaining real-world gap positions Chat2Workflow as a foundation for advancing industrial-grade automation. Code is available at https://github.com/zjunlp/Chat2Workflow.

AI.tldr: Introduces Chat2Workflow, a benchmark and agentic framework to automatically generate executable visual workflows from natural language using large language models, highlighting current limitations and modest gains.
AI.motivation: Manual construction of executable visual workflows is labor-intensive, slow, and error-prone, as developers must hand-design the workflow structure, craft prompts for each step, and continually update logic when requirements change. The authors want to investigate whether large language models can automate this multi-round interaction and workflow authoring process in a way that is reliable enough for real-world industrial use.
AI.method: They build Chat2Workflow, a benchmark derived from a large set of real-world business workflows, where each instance can be transformed and directly deployed to existing workflow platforms like Dify and Coze. On top of this benchmark, they design a robust agentic framework that iteratively interacts with and monitors the workflow generation process to mitigate recurrent execution errors and improve the correctness and stability of the produced visual workflows. They then evaluate state-of-the-art language models on this benchmark, both with and without their framework.
AI.result: Experiments show that current state-of-the-art language models can generally infer the high-level user intent from natural language, but they frequently fail to produce workflows that are fully correct, stable, and executable, particularly when requirements are complex or evolve over time. The proposed agentic framework improves the resolve rate by up to 5.34%, demonstrating some gains in reliability, but performance is still far from the level needed for seamless real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: Automatic generation of executable visual workflows from natural language is feasible in principle but remains challenging in practice: current LLMs struggle with correctness and robustness under realistic, changing requirements. Chat2Workflow provides a realistic benchmark and an initial agentic solution, exposing a significant performance gap that future research must close to achieve industrial-grade workflow automation. The work positions Chat2Workflow as a foundational resource for advancing this area.

title: Exploring Language-Agnosticity in Function Vectors: A Case Study in Machine Translation
summary: Function vectors (FVs) are vector representations of tasks extracted from model activations during in-context learning. While prior work has shown that multilingual model representations can be language-agnostic, it remains unclear whether the same holds for function vectors. We study whether FVs exhibit language-agnosticity, using machine translation as a case study. Across three decoder-only multilingual LLMs, we find that translation FVs extracted from a single English$\rightarrow$Target direction transfer to other target languages, consistently improving the rank of correct translation tokens across multiple unseen languages. Ablation results show that removing the FV degrades translation across languages with limited impact on unrelated tasks. We further show that base-model FVs transfer to instruction-tuned variants and partially generalize from word-level to sentence-level translation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether function vectors (task representations extracted from model activations) are language-agnostic in multilingual LLMs, focusing on machine translation.
AI.motivation: While multilingual models often have language-agnostic internal representations, it is unknown if higher-level task representations—function vectors derived from in-context learning—share this property. Understanding this matters for transfer, efficiency, and controllability of multilingual capabilities.
AI.method: Define function vectors (FVs) for translation by extracting activation differences associated with an English→Target translation task in decoder-only multilingual LLMs. Test whether these FVs improve translation for other unseen target languages by injecting them at inference. Conduct transfer experiments across languages, ablations where FVs are removed, and tests of transfer from base to instruction-tuned models and from word-level to sentence-level translation.
AI.result: Translation FVs learned from a single English→Target direction systematically enhance the rank of correct translation tokens for multiple other target languages, even ones not used to construct the FV. Ablations show that removing the FV harms translation quality across languages but has limited impact on unrelated tasks. Base-model FVs still work when applied to instruction-tuned versions and show partial generalization from word-level to sentence-level tasks.
AI.conclusion: Function vectors for translation are at least partly language-agnostic in multilingual decoder-only LLMs. A translation FV learned in one language pair can transfer across languages, models, and to longer-context translation, while remaining relatively specific to the translation function rather than broadly affecting other behaviors.

title: An Answer is just the Start: Related Insight Generation for Open-Ended Document-Grounded QA
summary: Answering open-ended questions remains challenging for AI systems because it requires synthesis, judgment, and exploration beyond factual retrieval, and users often refine answers through multiple iterations rather than accepting a single response. Existing QA benchmarks do not explicitly support this refinement process. To address this gap, we introduce a new task, document-grounded related insight generation, where the goal is to generate additional insights from a document collection that help improve, extend, or rethink an initial answer to an open-ended question, ultimately supporting richer user interaction and a better overall question answering experience. We curate and release SCOpE-QA (Scientific Collections for Open-Ended QA), a dataset of 3,000 open-ended questions across 20 research collections. We present InsightGen, a two-stage approach that first constructs a thematic representation of the document collection using clustering, and then selects related context based on neighborhood selection from the thematic graph to generate diverse and relevant insights using LLMs. Extensive evaluation on 3,000 questions using two generation models and two evaluation settings shows that InsightGen consistently produces useful, relevant, and actionable insights, establishing a strong baseline for this new task.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new QA task and dataset for generating document-grounded "related insights" that refine or extend initial answers to open-ended questions, and proposes InsightGen, a clustering-based LLM pipeline that performs well on this task.
AI.motivation: Open-ended question answering requires more than retrieving facts; it involves synthesis, judgment, and iterative refinement as users explore and adjust answers. Existing QA benchmarks do not model this iterative refinement process: they focus on single-shot answers and lack explicit support for generating follow-up content that helps users deepen or revise their understanding. The authors want to fill this gap by defining a task and resources that capture how AI systems can help users improve or rethink initial answers via additional, document-grounded insights.
AI.method: The authors define a new task called document-grounded related insight generation: given an initial answer to an open-ended question and a collection of documents, the system must generate further insights that improve, extend, or reconsider the answer while staying grounded in the documents. They build SCOpE-QA, a dataset of 3,000 open-ended questions covering 20 scientific research collections. They propose InsightGen, a two-stage pipeline. First, it builds a thematic representation of the document collection by clustering documents and constructing a thematic graph. Second, it performs neighborhood selection over this graph to pick related contexts and feeds these into large language models to generate diverse, relevant insights. They evaluate InsightGen using two different generation models and two evaluation settings on all 3,000 questions.
AI.result: InsightGen is shown to consistently generate insights that are useful, relevant to the question and initial answer, and actionable for refining or extending the user’s understanding. Across 3,000 questions, in both evaluated generation models and under two different evaluation setups, InsightGen forms a strong empirical baseline for the new task of document-grounded related insight generation.
AI.conclusion: The work formalizes a new task—document-grounded related insight generation—that better reflects the iterative, exploratory nature of open-ended question answering. By releasing the SCOpE-QA dataset and introducing the InsightGen method, the authors provide both a benchmark and a strong initial baseline. Their results indicate that structuring document collections thematically and using this structure to guide LLM-based generation can effectively produce high-quality, diverse, and actionable follow-up insights to improve the QA experience.

title: Epistemic orientation in parliamentary discourse is associated with deliberative democracy
summary: The pursuit of truth is central to democratic deliberation and governance, yet political discourse reflects varying epistemic orientations, ranging from evidence-based reasoning grounded in verifiable information to intuition-based reasoning rooted in beliefs and subjective interpretation. We introduce a scalable approach to measure epistemic orientation using the Evidence--Minus--Intuition (EMI) score, derived from large language model (LLM) ratings and embedding-based semantic similarity. Applying this approach to 15 million parliamentary speech segments spanning 1946 to 2025 across seven countries, we examine temporal patterns in discourse and its association with deliberative democracy and governance. We find that EMI is positively associated with deliberative democracy within countries over time, with consistent relationships in both contemporaneous and lagged analyses. EMI is also positively associated with the transparency and predictable implementation of laws as a dimension of governance. These findings suggest that the epistemic nature of political discourse is crucial for both the quality of democracy and governance.

AI.tldr: They develop a scalable metric (EMI) to quantify how much political speech relies on evidence versus intuition, then show that more evidence-based discourse correlates with better democratic deliberation and governance quality across countries and time.
AI.motivation: Democratic deliberation and effective governance rely on truth-seeking, but political discourse varies widely in how much it is grounded in verifiable evidence versus subjective intuition. Existing measures of this “epistemic orientation” of political speech are hard to scale across long time periods and multiple countries. The authors aim to create a large-scale, comparable way to quantify how evidence-based or intuition-based political discourse is, and to test whether this epistemic character is systematically related to the quality of democracy and governance.
AI.method: They construct an Evidence–Minus–Intuition (EMI) score for text segments by combining: (1) ratings from a large language model that assesses how much a speech segment reflects evidence-based versus intuition-based reasoning, and (2) embedding-based semantic similarity that places speech in a vector space aligned with these epistemic dimensions. They apply this measurement strategy to about 15 million parliamentary speech segments from 1946–2025 in seven countries. Using time-series and panel analyses within countries, they relate EMI to indicators of deliberative democracy and governance, including contemporaneous and lagged models to test temporal associations.
AI.result: The EMI score is successfully computed at scale for parliamentary speeches across seven countries and nearly eight decades. Empirically, higher EMI—indicating more evidence-based and less intuition-based discourse—is positively associated with higher levels of deliberative democracy within countries over time. This positive relationship holds consistently in both contemporaneous models (same period) and lagged models (EMI predicting future deliberative quality). Additionally, EMI is positively associated with governance indicators capturing transparency and the predictable implementation of laws.
AI.conclusion: The epistemic orientation of political discourse—how much it relies on evidence rather than intuition—is a measurable, large-scale property of speech and is systematically linked to democratic and governance outcomes. Their EMI metric provides a scalable tool for tracking these epistemic features over time and across countries. The findings support the view that more evidence-based parliamentary discourse is not merely stylistic; it is associated with stronger deliberative democracy and better governance, particularly regarding transparent and predictable law implementation.

title: Discovering a Shared Logical Subspace: Steering LLM Logical Reasoning via Alignment of Natural-Language and Symbolic Views
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) still struggle with multi-step logical reasoning. Existing approaches either purely refine the reasoning chain in natural language form or attach a symbolic solver as an external module. In this work, we instead ask whether LLMs contain a shared internal logical subspace that simultaneously aligns natural-language and symbolic-language views of the reasoning process. Our hypothesis is that this logical subspace captures logical reasoning capabilities in LLMs that are shared across views while remaining independent of surface forms. To verify this, we employ Canonical Correlation Analysis on the paired residual activations from natural-language and symbolic-language reasoning chains, learning a low-dimensional subspace with maximum cross-view correlation. Furthermore, we design a training-free approach that steers LLMs reasoning chain along this logical subspace, thereby leveraging the complementary reasoning signals from both views. Experiments on four logical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, improving accuracy by up to 11 percentage points and generalizing well on out-of-domain problems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to identify and steer a shared “logical subspace” inside LLMs that aligns natural-language and symbolic reasoning, significantly improving multi-step logical reasoning performance without additional training.
AI.motivation: LLMs are weak at multi-step logical reasoning, and current fixes are limited: they either refine natural-language chains-of-thought or bolt on separate symbolic solvers. This raises the question of whether there is an internal representation within LLMs that jointly captures both natural-language and symbolic reasoning in a model-internal, surface-form-independent way, which could be exploited without extra training.
AI.method: The authors hypothesize an internal logical subspace shared between natural-language and symbolic reasoning traces. They run paired natural-language and symbolic reasoning chains through the same LLM, collect corresponding residual activations, and apply Canonical Correlation Analysis to learn a low-dimensional subspace with maximal cross-view correlation. They then develop a training-free steering mechanism that nudges the model’s reasoning trajectory along this learned subspace at inference time, effectively fusing complementary signals from both views.
AI.result: On four logical reasoning benchmarks, steering LLM activations along the learned logical subspace yields substantial gains, boosting accuracy by up to 11 percentage points and maintaining strong performance on out-of-domain problems, suggesting the subspace captures generalizable reasoning structure.
AI.conclusion: LLMs appear to contain a shared internal logical subspace that aligns natural-language and symbolic reasoning. By identifying this subspace via cross-view CCA and steering activations within it, one can substantially enhance multi-step logical reasoning without additional training, and the learned subspace generalizes beyond the training distribution.
